Ayahs

إِذَا وَقَعَتِ ٱلْوَاقِعَةُ (١)
When the Imminent [Hour] befalls (1)
لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِهَا كَاذِبَةٌ (٢)
—there is no denying that it will befall— (2)
خَافِضَةٌ رَّافِعَةٌ (٣)
[it will be] lowering and exalting. (3)
إِذَا رُجَّتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ رَجًّا (٤)
When the earth is shaken violently, (4)
وَبُسَّتِ ٱلْجِبَالُ بَسًّا (٥)
and the mountains are shattered into bits (5)
فَكَانَتْ هَبَآءً مُّنۢبَثًّا (٦)
and become scattered dust, (6)
وَكُنتُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا ثَلَـٰثَةً (٧)
you will be three groups: (7)
فَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ (٨)
The People of the Right Hand—and what are the People of the Right Hand?! (8)
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ (٩)
And the People of the Left Hand—and what are the People of the Left Hand?! (9)
وَٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ ٱلسَّـٰبِقُونَ (١٠)
And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: (10)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ (١١)
they are the ones brought near [to Allah], (11)
فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (١٢)
[who will reside] in the gardens of bliss. (12)
ثُلَّةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٣)
A multitude from the former [generations] (13)
وَقَلِيلٌ مِّنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٤)
and a few from the latter ones. (14)

Content of Surah al-Waqi'ah

In Tarikh al-Qur'an, it is narrated from Ibn al-Nadim that Surah "al-Waqi'ah" is the forty-fourth Surah revealed to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله [^1]. It was preceded by Surah "Ta-Ha" and followed by Surah "al-Shu'ara".

As is evident from its tone, and as commentators have explicitly stated, this Surah was revealed in Mecca, although some have suggested that verses 81 and 82 were revealed in Medina; however, there is no evidence for this claim, nor is there any sign within the aforementioned verses to support it.

Surah al-Waqi'ah, as its name suggests, speaks of the Resurrection and its characteristics, and this theme is the central issue throughout all 96 verses of the Surah. However, from one perspective, the content of the Surah can be summarized into eight sections:

  1. The beginning of the emergence of the Resurrection and the severe and terrifying events coinciding with it.

  2. The grouping of human beings on that Day and their division into the "Companions of the Right" (Ashab al-Yamin), the "Companions of the Left" (Ashab al-Shimal), and the "Foremost" (Muqarrabun).

  3. A detailed discussion regarding the stations of the Muqarrabun and the various types of their rewards in Paradise.

  4. A detailed discussion regarding the second group, namely the Companions of the Right, and the various Divine blessings bestowed upon them.

  5. A significant discussion regarding the "Companions of the Left" and their painful punishments in Hell.

[^1]: Tarikh al-Qur'an, Abu Abd Allah Zanjani, page 59.

  1. Mentioning various proofs surrounding the issue of the Hereafter through the manifestation of Allah's power: the creation of man from a humble drop of semen, the manifestation of life in plants, the descent of rain, and the glow of fire—which are also considered signs of Monotheism (Tawhid).

  2. A depiction of the state of the death throes and the transition from this world to the next, which is itself among the precursors to the Resurrection.

  3. Another brief look at the reward and punishment of the believers and the disbelievers, ending the Surah with the name of the Great Lord.

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

Regarding the recitation of this Surah, many narrations have been mentioned in Islamic sources. Among them, we read in a hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites Surah al-Waqi'ah, it is recorded that he is not among the heedless" [^1]. This is because the verses of the Surah are so jarring and awakening that they leave no room for human negligence.

For this reason, we read in another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: when he was asked why the signs of old age had appeared so quickly on his blessed face, he replied:

"Surahs Hud, al-Waqi'ah, al-Mursalat, and 'Amma Yatasa'alun have made me old!" [^2] (Because...

[^1]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 212; Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 273. [^2]: Khisal al-Saduq, Chapter of Four, Hadith 10.

...in these Surahs, there are shocking reports of the Resurrection, the Day of Judgment, terrifying events, and the punishment of criminals, as well as moving stories of the fate of previous nations and the calamities that descended upon them).

In another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Whoever recites al-Waqi'ah every Friday night, Allah will love him and make him beloved to all people. He will never see misery in the world, nor poverty, nor destitution, nor any affliction from the afflictions of the world, and he will be among the companions of the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام" [^1]. In another hadith, it is mentioned that Uthman bin Affan visited Abdullah bin Mas'ud during the illness from which he passed away. He asked: "What is your ailment?" He replied: "My sins." He asked: "What do you desire?" He replied: "The mercy of my Lord!" He said: "If you agree, shall we bring a physician for you?" He replied: "The Physician has made me ill." He said: "If you wish, I shall order your stipend from the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) to be brought to you." He replied: "You did not give it to me on the day I was in need, and today when I am without need, you give it to me?!" He said: "Let it be for your daughters." He replied: "They also have no need, for I have instructed them to recite Surah 'al-Waqi'ah'. I heard the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله say: 'Whoever recites Surah al-Waqi'ah every night, destitution shall never strike him'" [^2].

[^1]: Thawab al-A'mal (as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 203). [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 212.

For this reason, in another narration, Surah al-Waqi'ah is called the Surah of "Wealth" (Ghina) [^1]. It is clear that such blessings cannot be obtained merely by the movement of the tongue; rather, recitation must be followed by thought and reflection, and subsequently by movement and action.

[^1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 27, p. 111.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ (1) لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِها كاذِبَةٌ (2) خافِضَةٌ رافِعَةٌ (3) إِذا رُجَّتِ الْأَرْضُ رَجًّا (4) وَ بُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا (5) فَكانَتْ هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا (6) وَ كُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً (7) فَأَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ (8) وَ أَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ (9) وَ السَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ (10) أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ (11) فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ (12) ثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ (13) وَ قَلِيلٌ مِنَ الْآخِرِينَ (14)

Commentary

The Great Event!

Matters related to the Resurrection in the Holy Quran are usually accompanied by the mention of great, revolutionary, and crushing events at its onset. This is clearly visible in many Surahs of the Quran that discuss the Resurrection. In this Surah, al-Waqi'ah, which revolves around the issue of the Hereafter, this same meaning is perfectly evident in its first verses.

At the beginning, He says: "When the Great Event of the Resurrection occurs" (إِذا وَقَعَتِ الْواقِعَةُ) [^1].

"There is no denying its occurrence" (لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِها كاذِبَةٌ). Because the events preceding it are so great and intense that their effects appear in every particle of the universe.

"Waqi'ah" (The Event) is a veiled reference to the issue of the Resurrection, and since its occurrence is certain, it is referred to as "al-Waqi'ah," and some have even counted it as one of the names of the Resurrection.

Some have taken the word "kadhibah" here in an infinitive sense, indicating that the occurrence of the Resurrection will be so manifest and clear that there will be no room for denial or dispute. Others have interpreted it in its literal sense as an active participle, saying that there will be no denier in the face of the occurrence of the Resurrection [^1].

[^1]: "Idha" is in the accusative case due to "adverbiality" (zarfiyyah), and its governor is "laysa" which appears in the second verse... (Kashshaf under the verse in question), but the first possibility is the most appropriate.

[^1]: The feminine gender of the "pronoun" is because it implies "nafs kadhibah" (a lying soul) or "qadiyyah kadhibah" (a false proposition)...

In any case, the Resurrection is not only accompanied by the transformation of the cosmos, but human beings are also transformed, as the next verse says: "It brings down [some] and raises up [others]!" (خافِضَةٌ رافِعَةٌ) [^2]. The arrogant tyrants and the high-seated oppressors fall, and the oppressed believers and the righteous stand upon the peak of honor. Those who were honored without merit become abased, and the deprived become honored without cause. A group falls into the depths of Hell, and another group takes their place in the highest heights of Paradise; such is the nature of a great and extensive Divine revolution! Therefore, in a narration from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, we read in the interpretation of this verse:

"Khafidhah: By Allah, it abases the enemies of Allah into the Fire; Rafi'ah: By Allah, it raises the friends of Allah to Paradise!" [^3]

Then, providing more description in this regard, He says: "This is when...

[^2]: "Khafidhah rafi'ah" is the predicate of a deleted subject, originally "hiya khafidhah rafi'ah." [^3]: Khisal, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 204.

...the earth is shaken with a [violent] shaking" (إِذا رُجَّتِ الْأَرْضُ رَجًّا).

This earthquake is so great and intense that "the mountains are crushed and crumbled" (وَ بُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا).

"And they become scattered dust" (فَكانَتْ هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا). "Rujjat" is from the root "rujj," meaning to move violently, and "rajrajah" refers to agitation.

"Bussat" is from the root "bass," which originally means to soften flour with water.

"Haba'" means dust, and "munbath" means scattered. Some have said "haba'" refers to those tiny dust particles suspended in the air that are normally invisible unless sunlight shines through a hole into a dark place.

Now one must consider how heavy that earthquake and explosion must be to be able to shatter the massive mountains—which are proverbial for their solidity and strength—such that they turn into scattered dust. The cry that rises from this great explosion is even more terrifying.

In any case, various expressions are seen in the verses of the Quran regarding the state of the mountains on the threshold of the Resurrection, which in fact show the different stages of the great explosion of the mountains.

Sometimes it says: "The mountains will be in motion" (At-Tur: 10). Sometimes it says: "The mountains are torn away" (Al-Mursalat: 10). Sometimes it says: "They are lifted and crushed with a single blow" (Al-Haqqah: 14). At times it says: they will turn into a "heap of shifting sand" (Al-Muzzammil: 14). And sometimes it says: "They become scattered dust!" (The verse under discussion). Finally, it says: "They will be like carded wool" spread in the air, of which only a color is seen (Al-Qari'ah: 5).

Of course, no one but Allah knows exactly how the course of these events will be. Perhaps they cannot be described with words or within the framework of our speech, but these meaningful indications all signify the magnitude of this great explosion.

After explaining the occurrence of this great event and the major Resurrection, He addresses the state of the people on that Day, and before anything else, He divides them into three groups, saying: "And you will be [divided into] three groups" (وَ كُنْتُمْ أَزْواجاً ثَلاثَةً). We know that "zawj" (pair/kind) is not always used for male and female genders; rather, it is also applied to things that are associated with one another. Since various classes of people are associated with each other in the Resurrection and the Gathering, they are referred to as "azwaj."

Regarding the first group, He says: "First are the Companions of the Right; what [greatness] are the Companions of the Right?!" (فَأَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ) [^1]. "Ashab al-Maymanah" refers to those whose record of deeds is given to their right hand, and this matter in the Resurrection is a symbol and a sign for the righteous believers and those who are saved, as has been mentioned many times in the verses of the Quran.

Alternatively, "Maymanah" is derived from the root "yumn," meaning prosperity and happiness; thus, the first group is the group of the prosperous and the happy.

Given that in the next verse He introduces the second group as "Ashab al-Mash'amah" (derived from "shu'm" meaning ill-omen), this latter interpretation is more suitable [^2]. The expression "What are the Companions of the Right?" is to express the reality that no limit or end can be imagined for their happiness and prosperity. This is the highest description possible in such cases, similar to when we say: "So-and-so is a man; what a man!"

Then, mentioning the second group, He adds: "And another group are the Companions of the Left; what [misery] are the Companions of the Left?!" (وَ أَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ ما أَصْحابُ الْمَشْئَمَةِ). A group of wretched, dark-fated, helpless, and destitute people whose records of deeds are given to their left hands, which is itself a sign and symbol of their dark fate, guilt, and crime.

The expression "What are the Companions of the Left?" here also reflects the ultimate degree of their misery and wretchedness.

[^1]: In the syntax of this sentence, several possibilities have been given... [^2]: Although in the following verses, instead of "Ashab al-Mash'amah," "Ashab al-Shimal" is used.

Finally, He describes the third group as such: "And the Foremost are the Foremost" (وَ السَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ) [^1].

"They are the ones brought near [to Allah]!" (أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ). The "Sabiqun" are those who are not only foremost in faith but are also ahead in good deeds and human virtues and ethics. They are the "models" and "examples" for the people, and the leaders and guides of creation; for this reason, they are the ones brought near to the threshold of the Great Allah.

Therefore, if some commentators have interpreted their being foremost only as being ahead in obedience to Allah, or the five daily prayers, or Jihad, or Hijrah, or repentance, and the like, each has focused on one aspect of this broad concept; otherwise, this word includes these and other matters of goodness and blessings. Furthermore, if in Islamic narrations "Sabiqun" is sometimes interpreted as four individuals—Abel, the Believer of the People of Pharaoh, Habib al-Najjar (each of whom was foremost in his own nation), and also the Commander of the Faithful Ali bin Abi Talib عليه السلام who was the first man to embrace Islam—it is in fact an explanation of its clearest instances and does not mean limiting the concept of the verse [^1]. In another hadith, it is mentioned that the Messenger of Allah said:

"Do you know who are the foremost toward the shade of Allah's grace on the Day of Resurrection?" The companions said: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: "They are those who, when the truth is given to them, they accept it; and when they are asked for the truth, they grant it; and they judge for the people as they judge for themselves!" [^2] In some narrations, it has also been interpreted as the Messengers and Prophets [^3]. In a hadith, we also read that Ibn Abbas says: I asked the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله about this verse, and he said: Gabriel told me:

"That refers to Ali عليه السلام and his followers (Shi'a); they are the foremost to Paradise, brought near to Allah by His honor for them" [^4]. But these are also in fact explanations of clear instances of the broad concept above—a concept that includes all the foremost in every nation and people.

[^1]: In the syntax of this verse and the next, many possibilities have been given... [^1]: This meaning is narrated in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام) (Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 215). [^2]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 27, p. 134. [^3]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 206. [^4]: Ibid, p. 209.

Then, in a short sentence, He clarifies the high station of the Muqarrabun, saying: "The Muqarrabun are in the Gardens of Bliss" (فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ) [^1]. The expression "Gardens of Bliss" includes all types of material and spiritual blessings of Paradise.

Additionally, this expression can indicate that the gardens of Paradise are solely centers of blessing, unlike the gardens of this world which are sometimes a means of livelihood and toil, just as the state of the Muqarrabun in the Hereafter differs from their state in this world; for their high station in this world is accompanied by responsibilities, whereas in the other abode, it is solely a source of blessing.

It is obvious that "closeness" (qurb) here means "closeness of station," not "closeness of location," for Allah has no location and is closer to us than ourselves.

The next verse refers to how their numbers are divided among past nations and this nation, saying: "A large group from the former nations" (ثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

"And a few from the later ones" (وَ قَلِيلٌ مِنَ الْآخِرِينَ). "Thullah," as Raghib says in al-Mufradat, originally means a gathered piece of wool, then it came to be used for a congregation or group.

Some also derive it from "thulla 'arshuhu" (his throne collapsed and his rule was cut off) and consider it to mean a "segment," and here, by the context of its contrast with "a few from the later ones," it means a large segment.

[^1]: "Fi Jannat al-Na'im" (preposition and object) may be related to "Muqarrabin"...

According to these two verses, a large group of the Muqarrabun are from the former nations, and only a few of them are from the nation of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله.

A question may arise here: how is this consistent with the extraordinary importance of the Islamic nation, given that Allah addressed them as the best nation and said: "You were the best nation..." (Al-Imran: 110)?

The answer to this question becomes clear by considering two points: First, the Muqarrabun are the same as the "Sabiqun" (the foremost in faith). It is certain that in the Islamic nation, the foremost in accepting Islam in the early period were a small group, the first of whom among men was Ali عليه السلام and among women was Khadijah. Meanwhile, the large number of previous prophets and the number of their nations, and the existence of the foremost in every nation, causes them to be greater in number.

Second, numerical superiority does not mean qualitative superiority. In other words, it is possible that the number of the "Sabiqun" of this nation is fewer, but they are much higher in station, just as there are differences among the prophets themselves: "Those messengers—some of them We caused to exceed others" (Al-Baqarah: 253).

It also seems necessary to mention that some believers may not be among the foremost in faith, but they possess other qualities and characteristics that place them in the same rank as the "Sabiqun," and they are equal to them in reward. Therefore, in some narrations from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, it is narrated that he said:

"We are the Foremost of the Foremost, and we are the Later ones" [^1].

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi under the verses in question.

And in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is mentioned that he addressed a group of his followers and said:

"You are the first Foremost, and the later Foremost, and the Foremost in the world toward our Wilayah, and in the Hereafter toward Paradise" [^1].

It is also worth reminding that some have interpreted the expression "former" and "later" as the "first of the Islamic nation" and the "last of the Islamic nation." According to this interpretation, all the Muqarrabun are from the Islamic nation.

However, this interpretation is consistent neither with the apparent meaning of the verses nor with the narrations that have been transmitted regarding these verses, which specifically introduce individuals from previous nations as the "former Foremost."

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi under the verses in question.

Ayahs

عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مَّوْضُونَةٍ (١٥)
On brocaded couches (15)
مُّتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَيْهَا مُتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (١٦)
reclining on them, face to face. (16)
يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدَٰنٌ مُّخَلَّدُونَ (١٧)
They will be waited upon by immortal youths, (17)
بِأَكْوَابٍ وَأَبَارِيقَ وَكَأْسٍ مِّن مَّعِينٍ (١٨)
with goblets and ewers and a cup of a clear wine, (18)
لَّا يُصَدَّعُونَ عَنْهَا وَلَا يُنزِفُونَ (١٩)
which causes them neither headache nor stupefaction, (19)
وَفَـٰكِهَةٍ مِّمَّا يَتَخَيَّرُونَ (٢٠)
and such fruits as they prefer (20)
وَلَحْمِ طَيْرٍ مِّمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ (٢١)
and such flesh of fowls as they desire, (21)
وَحُورٌ عِينٌ (٢٢)
and big-eyed houris (22)
كَأَمْثَـٰلِ ٱللُّؤْلُؤِ ٱلْمَكْنُونِ (٢٣)
like guarded pearls, (23)
جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٤)
a reward for what they used to do. (24)
لَا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا وَلَا تَأْثِيمًا (٢٥)
They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech, (25)
إِلَّا قِيلًا سَلَـٰمًا سَلَـٰمًا (٢٦)
but only the watchword, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ (26)

Commentary

The Heavenly Blessings Awaiting the Near-Stationed

These verses recount the various types of heavenly blessings bestowed upon the third group, namely the "Near-Stationed" (Muqarrabun). These are blessings, each more enchanting and soul-nourishing than the last, which can be summarized into seven categories:

First, He says: "They will be on thrones woven with gold and precious stones, lined up and joined together" (عَلى‌ سُرُرٍ مَوْضُونَةٍ).

"Reclining upon them, facing one another, in a gathering full of intimacy and joy" (مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلَيْها مُتَقابِلِينَ‌). "Surur" is the plural of "Sarir," derived from the root "Surur" (joy), referring to thrones upon which the possessors of blessings sit in gatherings of intimacy and happiness. [^1] "Mawdunah" is derived from "Wadn" (rhyming with Wazn), which originally means weaving a coat of mail. Subsequently, it was applied to any fabric whose warp and woof are firmly interwoven. Here, it refers to thrones placed perfectly side-by-side and joined together, or perhaps these thrones themselves have a special weave of pearls, rubies, and the like, as a group of commentators have stated.

In any case, the construction of these thrones, the manner of their arrangement, and the intimate gathering formed upon them—overflowing with delight and happiness—cannot be described by any words.

In the Holy Quran, interesting descriptions of heavenly thrones and the collective gatherings of the inhabitants of Paradise are frequently mentioned, indicating that one of their most significant pleasures is these sessions of intimacy and friendly circles. But what is the subject of their conversation and the talk of their gathering? No one knows for certain. Do they speak of the secrets of creation and the wonders of Allah's handiwork? Or of the principles of gnosis and His Beautiful Names and Attributes? Or of the events that occurred in this world? Or of the heart-wrenching calamities from which they are now relieved and at peace? Or other matters that we, in the conditions of this worldly life, are unable to comprehend? No one knows.

Then, speaking of their second blessing, He says: "Youths who remain forever in the splendor and freshness of adolescence shall go around them, serving them" (يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدانٌ مُخَلَّدُونَ‌). The expression "Yatufu" (from the root Tawaf) refers to their continuous service.

[^1]: Al-Mufradat by Raghib, under the root S-R.

And the expression "Mukhalladun" (immortalized)—even though all inhabitants of Paradise are immortal—refers to the immortality of their youthful vigor, freshness, and beauty.

Regarding who these youths are, various interpretations have been mentioned:

Some have said they are the children of the people of the world who passed away before reaching maturity. Since they had no good or evil deeds, by the grace of the Lord, they have attained such a position. Naturally, they derive the greatest pleasure from this task, as they are in the service of those stationed near to Allah.

This meaning has been narrated in a tradition from Ali عليه السلام.

However, in another interpretation, we read that these are the children of the polytheists who, due to their innocence, have attained such a rank, because the children of the believers are joined with their fathers and mothers.

In a third interpretation, we read that they are heavenly servants whom Allah created specifically for this purpose.

"These beautiful youths go around them with goblets, pitchers, and cups filled with pure wine drawn from flowing heavenly springs, quenching their thirst" (بِأَكْوابٍ وَ أَبارِيقَ وَ كَأْسٍ مِنْ مَعِينٍ‌). [^1]

[^1]: "Akwab" is the plural of "Kub," meaning a goblet or a vessel without a handle. "Abariq" is the plural of "Ibriq," originally derived from the Persian root "Abriz," referring to vessels that have a handle and a spout for pouring liquids. "Ka’s" refers to an overflowing cup, and "Ma’in" is from the root "Ma’n" (rhyming with Sahn), meaning flowing.

But it is not a wine that takes away the intellect and consciousness or brings about intoxication; rather, when the inhabitants of Paradise drink it, "they will neither suffer a headache from it, nor will they be intoxicated" (لا يُصَدَّعُونَ عَنْها وَ لا يُنْزِفُونَ‌). [^1] Only a state of indescribable spiritual ecstasy comes over them, immersing their entire being in an unparalleled pleasure!

Then, referring to the fourth and fifth parts of the material blessings of the Near-Stationed in Paradise, He says: "The heavenly youths present them with any kind of fruit they choose" (وَ فاكِهَةٍ مِمَّا يَتَخَيَّرُونَ‌). [^2]

"And the flesh of fowls, of whatever kind they desire" (وَ لَحْمِ طَيْرٍ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ‌). Mentioning fruit before meat is because it is better and superior from a nutritional standpoint; additionally, fruit before a meal has a different kind of pleasantness.

Of course, it is understood from some other verses of the Quran that the branches of heavenly trees are fully within reach of the inhabitants of Paradise, such that they can easily partake of any fruit personally. This meaning certainly applies to other heavenly foods as well. However, there is no doubt that when servants bring such foods in such a manner, it possesses a different grace and elegance. In other words, this is a form of greater respect and honor toward the inhabitants of Paradise and adds more vibrancy and charm to their intimate gatherings. Even in the common gatherings of this world, it often happens that despite fruit and food being within reach of the guests, the host personally offers them, and this is considered a form of respect and affection.

And naturally, among various types of meat, the flesh of birds is superior; hence it has been emphasized.

It is also worth noting that regarding fruit, the expression "Yatakhayyarun" (they choose) is used, and regarding meat, the expression "Yashtahun" (they desire) is used. Some commentators have sought to distinguish between these two expressions, but it seems more likely that both refer to the same meaning using two different phrases: the intent is that whatever kind of food the inhabitants of Paradise prefer is placed at their disposal by the heavenly servants.

Then, referring to the sixth blessing, which is pure and beautiful spouses, He says: "And they shall have spouses from the Hur al-’In" (وَ حُورٌ عِينٌ‌). [^1]

"Like pearls hidden in their shells!" (كَأَمْثالِ اللُّؤْلُؤِ الْمَكْنُونِ‌).

[^1]: Although some have imagined that "Hur ‘In" is coordinated with "Ghilman Mukhalladun" and thus they also circulate around the inhabitants of Paradise, given the inappropriateness of this meaning—especially with the collective gatherings of the inhabitants of Paradise—it is apparent that it is a subject for an omitted predicate, and the estimation is: wa lahum hurun ‘in ("and for them are Hur al-’In").

"Hur," as we have said before, is the plural of "Hawra’" and "Ahwar," referring to someone whose iris is intensely black and whose sclera is intensely white and transparent.

And "‘In" is the plural of "‘Ayna’" and "A’yan," meaning large-eyed. Since most of human beauty is in the eyes, this matter has been specifically emphasized.

Some have also said that "Hur" is derived from the root "Hayrat" (wonder), meaning they are so beautiful that eyes are bewildered by seeing them. [^1] "Maknun" means covered or hidden, and here it refers to being hidden in a shell; for a pearl, when it is in its shell and no hand has touched it, is more transparent and beautiful than ever. Additionally, it may refer to the fact that they are completely veiled from the eyes of others—no hand has touched them, and no eye has fallen upon them!

After mentioning these six physical blessings, He adds: "All this is a reward for the righteous deeds they used to do" (جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ‌). This is so it is not imagined that these countless heavenly blessings are given to anyone without account, or that the mere claim of faith and righteous action is sufficient to attain them. No, continuous and sincere action is necessary for these favors to be bestowed upon a person (note that "Ya’malun" is a present-tense verb and carries the meaning of continuity).

The seventh and final blessing, which is of a spiritual nature, is that: "They shall hear therein neither vain talk nor sinful speech"

[^1]: Abu al-Futuh al-Razi, Vol. 11, under the verse in question.

(لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً وَ لا تَأْثِيماً).

There is no lying, slander, or calumny there, nor mockery or backbiting, nor stinging words, nor ear-grating expressions, nor vain and baseless talk. Whatever exists there is grace, purity, beauty, dignity, etiquette, and sanctity. How sublime is an environment where tainted speech does not exist! If we think correctly, most of our distress in the life of this world also stems from such vain, useless, and sinful talk, and from the "wounds of the tongue."

Then He adds: "The only thing they hear there is 'Peace, Peace'!" (إِلَّا قِيلًا سَلاماً سَلاماً). [^1] Is this "Salam" from Allah? Or from the angels? Or from the inhabitants of Paradise to one another? Or all of these? The latter interpretation is the most appropriate, as other verses of the Quran refer to the "Salam" of Allah, the angels, and the inhabitants of Paradise to one another. [^2] Yes, they hear nothing but "Salam"—the peace and greetings of Allah and His near-stationed angels, and their own greetings to one another in those passionate and pure gatherings overflowing with friendship and love.

Their environment is saturated with peace and safety, and this very meaning governs their entire being.

[^1]: "Salama" is the object (maf'ul bihi) for "Qilan," which is a verbal noun like "Qawl," meaning their speech there is "Salam." It is also possible that "Salama" is an adjective for "Qilan," or an object for an omitted verb, estimated as "Yusallimuna salaman" (they greet with peace), but the first meaning is best, and the second "Salama" is for emphasis.

[^2]: Surah Ya-Sin, Verse 58; Ar-Ra'd, Verse 24; Yunus, Verse 10.

Whatever they say revolves around this axis, and the result of all their conversations and discussions leads to peace, harmony, and purity. Fundamentally, Paradise is "Dar al-Salam" (the Abode of Peace) and the house of health, safety, and security, as we read in Surah Al-An'am, Verse 127: "For them is the Abode of Peace with their Lord." [^1]

[^1]: Note that the exception in the verse "إِلَّا قِيلًا سَلاماً سَلاماً" is a "disconnected exception" (istithna' munqati') and serves to provide emphasis.

Ayahs

وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْيَمِينِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٢٧)
And the People of the Right Hand—what are the People of the Right Hand?! (27)
فِى سِدْرٍ مَّخْضُودٍ (٢٨)
Amid thornless lote trees (28)
وَطَلْحٍ مَّنضُودٍ (٢٩)
and clustered spathes (29)
وَظِلٍّ مَّمْدُودٍ (٣٠)
and extended shade, (30)
وَمَآءٍ مَّسْكُوبٍ (٣١)
and ever-flowing water (31)
وَفَـٰكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ (٣٢)
and abundant fruits, (32)
لَّا مَقْطُوعَةٍ وَلَا مَمْنُوعَةٍ (٣٣)
neither inaccessible, nor forbidden, (33)
وَفُرُشٍ مَّرْفُوعَةٍ (٣٤)
and noble spouses. (34)
إِنَّآ أَنشَأْنَـٰهُنَّ إِنشَآءً (٣٥)
We have created them with a special creation, (35)
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُنَّ أَبْكَارًا (٣٦)
and made them virgins, (36)
عُرُبًا أَتْرَابًا (٣٧)
loving, of a like age, (37)
لِّأَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٣٨)
for the People of the Right Hand. (38)
ثُلَّةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣٩)
A multitude from the former [generations] (39)
وَثُلَّةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٤٠)
and a multitude from the latter [ones]. (40)

Commentary

The Bounties and Blessings of the Companions of the Right

After describing the spiritual and material blessings of the Foremost (al-Muqarrabun), it is the turn of the "Companions of the Right" (Ashab al-Yamin)—that fortunate group whose record of deeds is given to their right hand as a sign of victory in Divine trials. Here, [the Quran] mentions six blessings from the blessings of Allah, which, when compared to the blessings of the Foremost (which were presented in seven parts), are one level lower.

First, to express the loftiness of their station, He says: "And the companions of the right—what are the companions of the right?" (وَ أَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ ما أَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ) [^1]. This is the highest description given of them, for this expression is used in cases where a person's attributes cannot be contained in words; in any case, this expression signifies the high status of the Companions of the Right.

The next verse points to the first gift of this group, saying: "They shall be amid thornless lote-trees" (فِي سِدْرٍ مَخْضُودٍ) [^2]. In truth, this is the most eloquent description possible for heavenly trees within the framework of our worldly terminology. For "Sidr" (lote-tree), according to some lexicographers, is a massive tree whose height sometimes reaches forty meters and is said to live up to two thousand years (having a very heavy and delicate shade). The only flaw of this tree is that it is thorny, but with the description "makhdud" (from the root khad meaning to cut or remove thorns), this problem is also resolved in the heavenly lote-trees.

In a Hadith, it is mentioned that whenever some vocabulary of the Quran became difficult for the companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), they would say that Allah benefits us through the blessings of the desert Arabs and their questions. Among these instances was a desert Arab who one day came to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and said: "O Messenger of Allah! Allah the Almighty has mentioned a harmful and hurting tree in the Quran, and I did not think such a tree would be in Paradise!" The Prophet asked: "Which tree?" He replied: "The lote-tree (Sidr), for it has thorns." The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: "Does Allah not say: 'فِي سِدْرٍ مَخْضُودٍ'? Its meaning is that He has cut off its thorns and placed a fruit in the place of every thorn, such that every fruit has seventy-two colors of nutrients, none of which resembles the other!" [^1]

The second gift is that "they reside in the shade of 'Talh' trees piled one above another" (وَ طَلْحٍ مَنْضُودٍ). "Talh" is a green tree with a beautiful color and pleasant scent. A group [of commentators] has said it refers to banana trees, which have very wide, green, and beautiful leaves, and sweet, wholesome fruit. "Mandud" is from the root nad meaning piled or clustered.

This expression may refer to the density of the leaves, the density of the fruits, or both. Some have even said these trees are so full of fruit that the trunks and branches are submerged in and covered by them.

Some commentators have noted that considering the lote-tree has very small leaves and the banana tree has very wide and large leaves, the mention of these two trees is a subtle reference to all the trees of Paradise that fall between these two [extremes]. [^2]

The third heavenly blessing is described as: "And extended shade" (وَ ظِلٍّ مَمْدُودٍ).

Some have interpreted this extended shade as a state similar to the time between dawn and sunrise, where shade covers everywhere. In a Hadith in Rawdat al-Kafi, this meaning is narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family). [^1] The purpose is that the heat of the sun never pains or bothers the inhabitants of Paradise, and they constantly live in pleasant, extended, and soul-refreshing shades.

In the fourth stage, referring to the waters of Paradise, He says: "The inhabitants of Paradise reside near waters like waterfalls (which have an exceptionally beautiful and delightful view)" (وَ ماءٍ مَسْكُوبٍ). "Maskub" is from the root sakb, originally meaning pouring. Since the pouring of water from above to below in the form of a waterfall creates the best scenery, its murmurs soothe the ear of the soul and its sight gives light to the eye; this has been made one of the gifts for the inhabitants of Paradise.

And certainly, those trees and all that constantly flowing water are accompanied by various fruits. Therefore, in the fifth blessing, He adds: "And abundant fruit" (وَ فاكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ), which is neither cut off nor ever forbidden (لا مَقْطُوعَةٍ وَ لا مَمْنُوعَةٍ).

Yes, it is not like the fruits of this world which are limited to certain seasons and appear on the tree for only a few weeks or months a year. Nor is it like the fruits of this world where sometimes thorns prevent picking them, or sometimes the dangerous height of the tree (like the date palm) is an obstacle, or an obstacle exists within the person himself from consuming it. Nor do the primary Host of Paradise, who is Allah the All-Bountiful, or His officials, have any stinginess or prohibition. Therefore, there is no obstacle at all; rather, "the cause is fully present and the obstacle in every shape and form is absent."

Then He points to another blessing, adding: "They have high-valued spouses" (وَ فُرُشٍ مَرْفُوعَةٍ). "Furush" is the plural of "firash," which originally means any kind of carpet or bedding that is spread out. By extension, it is sometimes used as a metaphor for a spouse (whether male or female).

Hence, in the famous Hadith from the Noble Prophet, it is said: "The child belongs to the bed (firash), and for the adulterer is the stone" (meaning a child born to a married woman belongs to that family, and if there was a profligate man involved, his share is only disappointment/punishment, and the possibility of the child being from his seed is not accepted).

Some have also interpreted "furush" in its literal sense (not metaphorical) and considered it a reference to the very precious and valuable carpets and bedding of Paradise. However, in that case, the connection with the subsequent verses, which speak of the Houris and heavenly spouses, would be severed.

Then He proceeds to other attributes of the heavenly spouses, saying: "Indeed, We have produced them in a [new] creation" (إِنَّا أَنْشَأْناهُنَّ إِنْشاءً).

This sentence may refer to the spouses of the believers in this world, whom Allah gives a new creation in the Resurrection, and they all enter Paradise in the ultimate state of youth, freshness, beauty, and outward and inward perfection—for the nature of Paradise is the nature of perfection and departure from every defect and flaw.

And if the reference is to the Houris, Allah has given them a new creation such that the dust of old age and weakness never settles upon them. It is possible that the expression "insha" (production/creation) refers to both.

Then He adds: "And We made them virgins" (فَجَعَلْناهُنَّ أَبْكاراً). Perhaps this attribute remains with them forever, as many commentators have explicitly stated and as mentioned in narrations; meaning, their state does not change with intimacy. [^1]

And in describing them, He further adds: "Devoted to their husbands and eloquent" (عُرُباً). "Urub" is the plural of "arubah," meaning a woman whose state indicates her love for her husband and her status of chastity and purity. This is because "i'rab" means making manifest. This word also carries the meaning of being eloquent and well-spoken, and it is possible that both meanings are combined in the verse.

Another of their attributes is that "they are of equal age with their spouses and are all alike in goodness and outward and inward beauty, each better than the other" (أَتْراباً).

"Atrab" is the plural of "tirb," meaning a peer or equal. Some have said this meaning is derived from "tara'ib," which refers to the ribs of the chest, because they resemble one another.

This similarity and equality may be in age relative to their spouses, so that, so to speak, they fully understand each other's feelings, and living together is more delightful for them—though this can sometimes occur with age differences, it is generally not the case. Or it may mean they are alike in goodness, beauty, and outward and inward excellence, exactly like the famous expression: "They are all good, and each is better than the other."

Then He adds: "All these blessings are for the companions of the right" (لِأَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ). This is a renewed emphasis on the exclusivity of these (six) bounties to them.

The possibility has also been raised that this sentence completes the phrase "إِنَّا أَنْشَأْناهُنَّ إِنْشاءً", meaning: "We have produced them in a new creation for the companions of the right." [^1]

And at the end of this discourse, He says: "A large company of them are from the first generations" (ثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

"And a large company from the later generations" (وَ ثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْآخِرِينَ).

"Thullah" originally means a gathered piece of wool, then it was applied to any dense, large crowd. Accordingly, a great group of the Companions of the Right are from past nations, and a great group are from the Islamic nation, because the righteous and believers are many within this nation—even if their "foremost" (as-sabiqun) in accepting faith are fewer compared to the foremost of previous nations, given the vast number of those nations and their prophets.

Some have said both these groups are from the Islamic nation—a group from the first of them and a group from the last of them—but the first interpretation is more correct.

Ayahs

وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلشِّمَالِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلشِّمَالِ (٤١)
And the People of the Left Hand—what are the People of the Left Hand?! (41)
فِى سَمُومٍ وَحَمِيمٍ (٤٢)
Amid infernal miasma and boiling water (42)
وَظِلٍّ مِّن يَحْمُومٍ (٤٣)
and the shadow of a dense black smoke, (43)
لَّا بَارِدٍ وَلَا كَرِيمٍ (٤٤)
neither cool nor beneficial. (44)
إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَبْلَ ذَٰلِكَ مُتْرَفِينَ (٤٥)
Indeed they had been affluent before this, (45)
وَكَانُوا۟ يُصِرُّونَ عَلَى ٱلْحِنثِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٤٦)
and they used to persist in the great sin. (46)
وَكَانُوا۟ يَقُولُونَ أَئِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ (٤٧)
And they used to say, ‘What! When we are dead and become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected?! (47)
أَوَءَابَآؤُنَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ (٤٨)
And our forefathers too?!’ (48)
قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٤٩)
Say, ‘Indeed the former and latter generations (49)
لَمَجْمُوعُونَ إِلَىٰ مِيقَـٰتِ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (٥٠)
will all be gathered for the tryst of a known day. (50)

Commentary

The Painful Punishments of the People of the Left

Following the discussion on the magnificent blessings of the "Foremost" (Muqarrabun) and the "People of the Right" (Ashab al-Yamin), the discourse now turns to the third group and their painful and terrifying punishments, so that through comparison, the state of all three groups becomes clear.

It says: "And the People of the Left—what of the People of the Left?" (وَ أَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ ما أَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ‌).

These are the ones whose record of deeds will be given to their left hand, which is a symbol of them being sinners, defiled, oppressors, and inhabitants of Hell. As mentioned in the description of the Foremost and the People of the Right, this expression is used to convey the ultimate degree of a person's good or bad state. For example, we say: "A fortune came our way—what a fortune!" or "A calamity befell us—what a calamity!"

Then, referring to three types of their punishments, it says: "They will be amid scorching winds and scalding water" (فِي سَمُومٍ وَ حَمِيمٍ‌).

"And in the shadow of black smoke" (وَ ظِلٍّ مِنْ يَحْمُومٍ‌).

The lethal scorching wind from one side, the deadly boiling water from another, and the shadow of hot, suffocating smoke from a third side, will so entrap them that it strips away all their strength and endurance. Even if they had no other affliction but these three, it would suffice for their punishment.

"Samum" (سَمُوم) is derived from the root "Samm" (سَمّ), meaning a scorching wind that enters the "Masamm" (the tiny pores of the human body) and destroys it. (In principle, poison is called "Samm" because it penetrates all particles of the body).

"Hamim" (حَمِيم) means something hot, and here it refers to scalding water, which is also mentioned in other verses of the Quran, such as Surah Al-Hajj, verse 19: يُصَبُّ مِنْ فَوْقِ رُؤُسِهِمُ الْحَمِيمُ‌: "Scalding water will be poured over their heads."

"Yahmum" (يَحْمُوم) is also from the same root, and here, in relation to "Zill" (shadow), it is interpreted as thick, black, and hot smoke.

Then, for emphasis, it adds: "A shadow neither cool nor refreshing" (لا بارِدٍ وَ لا كَرِيمٍ‌). A canopy sometimes protects a person from the sun, and sometimes from wind and rain, or provides other benefits; but it is evident that this canopy possesses none of these benefits.

The term "Karim" (كَرِيم) is derived from "Karamah" (dignity/generosity), meaning something beneficial. Therefore, it is common among Arabs that when they want to describe a person or thing as useless, they say: "La karamata fih" (There is no dignity/benefit in it). Certainly, from a shadow made of black and suffocating smoke, nothing but evil and harm can be expected—"La karamah."

Although the punishments of the inhabitants of Hell are of various and terrifying kinds, the mention of these three parts is sufficient for a person to infer the rest.

In the following verses, the reasons for the People of the Left falling into this grim and terrifying fate are summarized in three initial sentences: First: "Indeed, before that, they lived in luxury and were intoxicated by blessings" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَبْلَ ذلِكَ مُتْرَفِينَ‌).

"Mutraf" (مُتْرَف), as stated in Lisan al-Arab, is derived from "Taraf" (with the weight of sabab), meaning living in luxury. A mutraf is someone whom the abundance of blessings has made heedless, arrogant, and intoxicated, leading them to rebellion [^1]. It is true that not all "People of the Left" are among the "Mutrafin," but the Quran's target is their leaders.

As we see today, the corruption of human societies stems from the group of intoxicated and arrogant wealthy elites who are also the cause of others' deviation. The threads of all wars, bloodshed, various crimes, centers of lust, and deviant inclinations are in the hands of this group; for this reason, the Quran points its finger at them before anything else.

There is also the possibility that "blessing" (ni'mah) has a broad meaning and is not limited to wealth, but rather youth, health, and life are also blessings from Allah which, if they cause arrogance and heedlessness, become the primary source of sins. Each of the People of the Left possesses some form of these blessings.

Then, pointing to their second sin, it adds: "And they used to persist in the great sin"

[^1]: Lisan al-Arab, Vol. 9, p. 17.

(وَ كانُوا يُصِرُّونَ عَلَى الْحِنْثِ الْعَظِيمِ‌).

"Hinth" (حِنْث) originally means any kind of sin, but in many instances, this word is used to mean breaking a covenant or violating an oath, because these are clear examples of sin.

Therefore, the characteristic of the People of the Left is not merely committing a sin, but rather persisting in "great sins." This is because a sin might occasionally be committed by the People of the Right as well, but they never persist in it; when they are reminded, they immediately repent.

A group has interpreted "Hinth al-Azim" here as "Shirk" (polytheism), because no sin is greater than that, as the Quran says: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لا يَغْفِرُ أَنْ يُشْرَكَ بِهِ وَ يَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ: "Indeed, Allah does not forgive associating partners with Him, but He forgives what is less than that for whom He wills" [^1]. Some have also interpreted it as "lying," which is among the greatest sins and the key to other transgressions, especially when coupled with the denial of the Prophets and the Resurrection.

However, it appears that all of these are considered instances of "Hinth al-Azim."

And their third wrongful act was that they used to say: "When we have died and become dust and bones, shall we indeed be resurrected?" (وَ كانُوا يَقُولُونَ أَ إِذا مِتْنا وَ كُنَّا تُراباً وَ عِظاماً أَ إِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ‌). Therefore, the denial of the Resurrection—which itself is the source of many sins—is another attribute of the People of the Left. The expression "they used to say" (كانُوا يَقُولُونَ) indicates that they were also persistent and stubborn in their denial of the Resurrection.

Two points are noteworthy here: First: When the "People of the Right" and the "Foremost" were discussed, the details of the deeds that led to those rewards were not given (except for a brief reference regarding the Foremost). However, when it comes to the "People of the Left," a sufficient explanation is provided, both to establish the proof (itmam al-hujjah) and to state the reality that those painful punishments are completely consistent with the principle of justice.

Second: The three sins mentioned in the three verses above can, in fact, refer to the denial of the three principles of religion by the People of the Left: the last verse was the denial of the Resurrection, the second verse was the denial of Monotheism (Tawhid), and the first verse, which spoke of the "Mutrafin," is an allusion to the denial of the Prophets. For as we read in Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 23: وَ كَذلِكَ ما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَذِيرٍ إِلَّا قالَ مُتْرَفُوها إِنَّا وَجَدْنا آباءَنا عَلى‌ أُمَّةٍ وَ إِنَّا عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ مُقْتَدُونَ‌: "And similarly, We did not send before you any warner to a city except that its affluent ones said, 'Indeed, we found our fathers upon a religion, and we are following in their footsteps.'"

The expression "dust and bones" (ترابا و عظاما) may refer to the fact that our flesh turns to dust and our bones become bare; in such a state, how is a new creation possible? And because the distance of dust from new life is greater, it is mentioned first.

It is strange that they saw scenes of the Resurrection with their own eyes in this world—how many living beings like plants rot and become dust and then once again put on the garment of life. In principle, for Him who brought about the first creation, how could its repetition be difficult? Nevertheless, they constantly persisted in denying the Resurrection [^1].

[^1]: Note that the repetition of the interrogative particle and the use of "inna" are all brought for emphasis.

They were not content with even this and, to express further amazement, they would say: "And our forefathers of old?" (أَ وَ آباؤُنَا الْأَوَّلُونَ‌) [^2]—those of whom perhaps not a trace remains, or every particle of whose dust has fallen into a corner or become part of another creature's body?

However, as explained in detail at the end of Surah Ya-Sin, in the face of the firm proofs that indicate the matter of the Resurrection, these are a handful of hollow excuses.

Then the Quran commands the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله to say in response to them: "Say: Indeed, the former and the latter..." (قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَ الْآخِرِينَ‌).

"Will surely be gathered for the appointment of a known Day (the Day of Resurrection)" (لَمَجْمُوعُونَ إِلى‌ مِيقاتِ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ‌) [^3]. "Miqat" (مِيقات) is derived from "Waqt" (time), meaning a time designated for a task or a promise. What is meant by Miqat here is the appointed time of the Resurrection, on which day all humans will be gathered in the Assembly for the auditing of their accounts. Sometimes it is also used metaphorically for a place designated for performing a task, such as the "Miqats of Hajj," which are the names of specific places from which one dons the Ihram.

Furthermore, from the various expressions in this verse, several emphases regarding the Resurrection are derived (the use of "Inna," the "Lam" of emphasis, "Majmu'un" as a passive participle, and describing the "Day" as being "known").

From this verse, it is clearly understood that the Resurrection of all humans takes place together on a single day, and this same meaning has appeared in other verses of the Quran [^1]. From this, it becomes clear that those who have imagined the Resurrection to occur at multiple times for each nation separately are completely estranged from the verses of the Quran.

It perhaps does not need mentioning that the meaning of the Resurrection being "known" is that it is known to the Lord; otherwise, no one—not even the sent Prophets or the archangels—is aware of its timing.

[^1]: Surah Hud, verse 102; Surah Maryam, verse 95. [^2]: The "Hamza" in "أ و آبائنا الاولين" is the interrogative Hamza, and the "Waw" is the conjunctive "Waw" (and), which the interrogative Hamza precedes. [^3]: "Ila" (إلى) is used in this sentence because the Resurrection is at the end of this world; it is also possible that "Ila" here means "Lam" (for), just as "Li-miqat" appears in many verses of the Quran.

Ayahs

ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلضَّآلُّونَ ٱلْمُكَذِّبُونَ (٥١)
Then indeed, you, astray deniers, (51)
لَـَٔاكِلُونَ مِن شَجَرٍ مِّن زَقُّومٍ (٥٢)
will surely eat from the Zaqqum tree (52)
فَمَالِـُٔونَ مِنْهَا ٱلْبُطُونَ (٥٣)
and stuff your bellies with it, (53)
فَشَـٰرِبُونَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْحَمِيمِ (٥٤)
and drink boiling water on top of it, (54)
فَشَـٰرِبُونَ شُرْبَ ٱلْهِيمِ (٥٥)
drinking like thirsty camels.’ (55)
هَـٰذَا نُزُلُهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلدِّينِ (٥٦)
Such will be the hospitality they receive on the Day of Retribution. (56)

Commentary

Another Part of the Punishments for These Astray Criminals

These verses continue the discussion regarding the punishments of the "Companions of the Left" (Ashab al-Shimal), addressing them by saying: "Then indeed, O you who are astray and deniers,"

(ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ أَيُّهَا الضَّالُّونَ الْمُكَذِّبُونَ).

"Will surely eat from the tree of Zaqqum," (لَآكِلُونَ مِنْ شَجَرٍ مِنْ زَقُّومٍ) [^1]

"And will fill your bellies with it." (فَمالِؤُنَ مِنْهَا الْبُطُونَ).

In the preceding verses, only the living environment of the "Companions of the Left" in Hell was mentioned. However, here the discussion turns to their food and drink, which in this regard as well, stands in direct contrast to the Near Ones (Muqarrabun) and the Companions of the Right (Ashab al-Yamin).

It is noteworthy that the addressees in these verses are the "astray deniers"—those who, in addition to being lost and in error, possess a spirit of enmity and stubbornness against the Truth and continuously persist in this behavior.

"Zaqqum," as we have mentioned previously, is a plant that is bitter, foul-smelling, and foul-tasting, possessing a sap that causes the human body to swell upon contact. Sometimes it is used to refer to any type of loathsome food given to the inhabitants of Hell [^2] (For a more detailed explanation regarding "Zaqqum," refer to Volume 19, page 70, under verse 62 of Surah As-Saffat, and Volume 21, under verse 43 of Surah Ad-Dukhan).

The expression "فَمالِؤُنَ مِنْهَا الْبُطُونَ" (filling their bellies with it) indicates that they will first be afflicted with a state of intense hunger, such that they greedily eat from this highly unpleasant food and fill their stomachs.

[^1]: The "min" in "min shajarin" is partitive (tab'idiyyah), and in "min zaqqumin" it is explanatory (bayaniyyah).

[^2]: Majma' al-Bahrayn, Mufradat al-Raghib, Lisan al-Arab, and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani.

When they have eaten from this unpleasant food, they become thirsty. But what is their drink? The Quran says in the next verse: "And drink scalding water on top of it!" (فَشارِبُونَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْحَمِيمِ) [^1]

"And you will drink it greedily, like the drinking of camels afflicted with the disease of insatiable thirst." (فَشارِبُونَ شُرْبَ الْهِيمِ).

A camel afflicted with this disease becomes so thirsty and drinks water so continuously that it eventually perishes. Yes, such is the fate of the "astray deniers" on the Day of Resurrection.

"Hamim" (حميم) means water that is extremely hot and scalding; hence, warm and affectionate friends are called "Wali Hamim," and the word "Hammam" (bathhouse) is also derived from this same root.

"Him" (هيم - rhyming with 'mim') is the plural of "Ha'im" (some consider it the plural of "Ahyam" and "Hayma'"). It originally comes from "Hiyam" (rhyming with 'Furat'), meaning a thirst-sickness that affects camels. This expression is also used for burning passions and restless lovers.

Some commentators interpret "Him" to mean sandy lands that absorb whatever water is poured upon them, as if they are never quenched.

In the final verse under discussion, it refers once more to this food and drink, saying: "This is their hospitality on the Day of Recompense!" (هذا نُزُلُهُمْ يَوْمَ الدِّينِ).

[^1]: It is noteworthy that in the previous verse, the feminine pronoun (minha) refers back to "shajarin min zaqqumin," while in this verse, the masculine pronoun (alayhi) is used, because "shajar" (tree) and "thamar" (fruit) can be treated as both masculine and feminine (Majma' al-Bayan under the verse).

This is while the "Companions of the Right" rest in very delicate and refreshing shades, drinking from the best fruits, springs of pleasant water, and pure wine (sharaban tahura), intoxicated by the love of Allah. "See how vast the difference is between the two paths!"

The word "Nuzul," as we have said before, refers to the means by which a dear guest is welcomed, and sometimes it is applied to the first food or drink brought for a guest. Obviously, the inhabitants of Hell are not guests, nor are Zaqqum and Hamim considered means of hospitality; rather, this is a form of sarcasm directed at them, so they may realize that if this is their "welcome," then woe to their actual punishment and penalty!

Ayahs

نَحْنُ خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ فَلَوْلَا تُصَدِّقُونَ (٥٧)
We created you. Then why do you not acknowledge it? (57)
أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تُمْنُونَ (٥٨)
Have you considered the sperm that you emit? (58)
ءَأَنتُمْ تَخْلُقُونَهُۥٓ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُونَ (٥٩)
Is it you who create it, or are We the creator? (59)
نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنَا بَيْنَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتَ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ (٦٠)
We have ordained death among you, and We are not to be outmaneuvered (60)
عَلَىٰٓ أَن نُّبَدِّلَ أَمْثَـٰلَكُمْ وَنُنشِئَكُمْ فِى مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ (٦١)
from replacing you with your likes and recreating you in [a realm] you do not know. (61)
وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ ٱلنَّشْأَةَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ فَلَوْلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٦٢)
Certainly you have known the first genesis, then why do you not take admonition? (62)

Commentary

Seven Arguments for the Issue of Resurrection

Since the preceding verses spoke of those who deny the Resurrection, and fundamentally, the focus of the discussions in this Surah is primarily on proving the issue of Resurrection, these verses engage in a discussion and examination of the evidences for Resurrection. Altogether, it presents seven arguments for this important matter, which strengthen the foundations of faith in this regard and reassure the human heart concerning the Divine promises mentioned in the previous verses regarding the "Foremost" (Muqarrabun), the "Companions of the Right" (Ashab al-Yamin), and the "Companions of the Left" (Ashab al-Shimal).

In the first stage, It says: "We created you, so why do you not credit the second creation?" (نَحْنُ خَلَقْناكُمْ فَلَوْ لا تُصَدِّقُونَ‌) [^1] Why are you amazed by the Resurrection and the physical return after the body has turned to dust?

Did We not create you from dust on the first day? Is the "ruling of likes" (hukm al-amthal) not one and the same?

This reasoning is, in fact, similar to what appears in verses 78-79 of Surah Ya-Sin, where the Quran, in response to one of the polytheists who held a decayed bone in his hand and said, "Who can give life to these bones?" says:

وَ ضَرَبَ لَنا مَثَلًا وَ نَسِيَ خَلْقَهُ قالَ مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَ هِيَ رَمِيمٌ قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَها أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ وَ هُوَ بِكُلِّ خَلْقٍ عَلِيمٌ‌: "And he presents for Us an example and forgets his [own] creation. He says, 'Who will give life to bones while they are disintegrated?' Say, 'He will give them life who produced them the first time; and He is, of all creation, Knowing.'"

In the following verse, It points to the second argument, saying: "Have you considered the semen that you emit [into the womb]?" (أَ فَرَأَيْتُمْ ما تُمْنُونَ‌) [^1]

"Is it you who create it throughout the embryonic stages through repeated creations? Or are We the Creator?" (أَ أَنْتُمْ تَخْلُقُونَهُ أَمْ نَحْنُ الْخالِقُونَ‌). Who transforms this worthless and insignificant drop of fluid into a new form every day, giving it creation after creation and fashioning after fashion? Truly, are these wondrous developments—which have sparked the amazement of all people of understanding and thinkers—from your side or from Allah? Is One who has the power over these repeated creations unable to revive the dead on the Day of Resurrection?! This verse is, in fact, similar to verse 5 of Surah Al-Hajj, which says: "O people, if you should be in doubt about the Resurrection, then [consider that] indeed, We created you from dust, then from a sperm-drop, then from a clinging clot, and then from a lump of flesh, formed and unformed—that We may show you [Our power]. And We settle in the wombs whom We will for a specified term, then We bring you out as a child."

Beyond this, if we consider what today's scientists have discovered about this seemingly insignificant drop of water, the matter becomes clearer. They say that what causes the production of a human embryo is a combination of "sperm" (male seed) with "ovum" (female seed). The "sperms" are tiny microscopic organisms, and in every single ejaculation, there may be between two hundred to five hundred million sperms! (That is, equal to the population of several countries of the world!) [^1] And how wondrous it is that this tiny being, after combining with the female egg, grows rapidly and multiplies at a staggering rate to build the cells of the human body. Although the cells are seemingly all similar, they soon differentiate; one group forms the human heart, another the hands and feet, and another the ears and eyes, and each is placed exactly in its proper position. Neither do the heart cells go to the place of the kidneys, nor the kidney cells to the place of the heart; neither are the ear cells situated in the location of the eye cells, nor vice versa. In short, the embryo passes through tumultuous worlds during the gestation period until it is born as a child, and all of this is under the light of continuous and ongoing Divine creativity, while the simple role of man in this creation ends in just one moment—and that is the moment of emitting the seed into the womb, and nothing more! Is this not itself a living proof for the issue of Resurrection? That such an Exalted Power has the ability to revive the dead?! [^2]

Then, proceeding to state the third argument, It says: "We have decreed death among you, and We are not to be outrun" (نَحْنُ قَدَّرْنا بَيْنَكُمُ الْمَوْتَ وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ‌). Indeed, We shall never be defeated, and if We have decreed death, it is not because We are unable to grant eternal life.

Rather, the goal was "that We may replace your likes and bring you into being in [a form] that you do not know" (عَلى‌ أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ أَمْثالَكُمْ وَ نُنْشِئَكُمْ فِي ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌). In the commentary of these two verses, there exists another view besides what we mentioned above, which is that the second verse is not a statement of the goal of the first verse but its continuation, saying:

We are never helpless or defeated in removing one group and making another group their successors. [^1] Regarding the phrase "عَلى‌ أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ أَمْثالَكُمْ" (that We may replace your likes), there are also two interpretations: one is the same interpretation mentioned above, which is famous among commentators, according to which the talk is about the replacement of nations in this world. The second interpretation says: "likes" refers to the humans themselves who return on the Day of Resurrection, and the expression "like" is used because the human does not return with all [previous] characteristics, but will be in another time and with new qualities in terms of body and soul.

However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate. In any case, the goal is to present an argument for the Resurrection from the issue of death. The argument can be explained as follows: The Wise Allah, who created humans while one group constantly dies and another group succeeds them, had a purpose. If this purpose were only the life of this world, it would be fitting for human life to be eternal, not so short and mixed with thousands of hardships that it is not worth the coming and going.

Therefore, the law of death clearly testifies that this place is a passage, not a dwelling; a bridge, not a destination. For if it were the destination and dwelling, it ought to have endured.

The phrase "وَ نُنْشِئَكُمْ فِي ما لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌" (and bring you into being in forms that you do not know) apparently refers to the creation of man in the Resurrection, which can be the goal for the death and life of this world.

Naturally, since no one has seen the Hereafter, they are unaware of the principles and systems governing it; even the expression of its reality does not fit within the mold of our words; we only see a shadow of it from afar.

Furthermore, the above verse fully expresses this reality: that We create you in a new world and with new forms and conditions of which you have no knowledge. [^1]

In the last verse under discussion, the talk is of the fourth argument for Resurrection, saying: "And you have already known the first creation (this world), so why do you not take heed that there is another creation and world after it?" (وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولى‌ فَلَوْ لا تَذَكَّرُونَ‌). This argument can be stated in two ways: First, for example, if we pass through a desert and see in it a very magnificent and grand palace built with the strongest and finest materials and vast, extensive facilities, and then we are told that all these facilities and this massive building are only for a small caravan to rest in for a few hours and then leave, we would say to ourselves that this is not a wise act, because for such a purpose, it would have been appropriate to set up a few small tents.

A world of this greatness, with all these planets, the sun, the moon, and various earthly creatures, cannot have been created for a small goal like the few days of human life in the world; otherwise, the creation of the world would be vain and futile. These massive arrangements were created for a noble being like man so that he may know the Great God through them—a knowledge that is his great capital in the other life.

This statement is, in fact, similar to what appears in verse 27 of Surah Sad regarding the Resurrection: وَ ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما باطِلًا ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا: "And We did not create the heaven and the earth and that between them aimlessly. That is the assumption of those who disbelieve."

Secondly, you see the scenes of Resurrection in this world in every corner with your own eyes. Every year, the scene of Resurrection is repeated in the world of plants; He revives the dead lands with the descent of life-giving drops of rain, just as He says in verse 39 of Surah Fussilat: إِنَّ الَّذِي أَحْياها لَمُحْيِ الْمَوْتى‌ "Indeed, He who gives it life is the Giver of Life to the dead!" In verse 6 of Surah Al-Hajj, the same meaning is also pointed out.

Key Point: An Argument for Proving the Validity of Analogy (Qiyas)

In our Principles of Jurisprudence (Usul al-Fiqh), the issue is raised that the rulings of Sharia cannot be proven through analogy (Qiyas). For example, we cannot say that because a menstruating woman makes up her fasts, she must also make up her prayers (and so to speak, one must move from the universal to the particular, not from one particular to another), although Sunni scholars generally accept analogy as one of the sources of legislation in Islamic jurisprudence, while a group of them agrees with us on the issue of denying the validity of analogy.

Interestingly, some proponents of analogy have sought to argue for their purpose from the above verse (وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولى‌ فَلَوْ لا تَذَكَّرُونَ‌)! Because Allah says: now compare the state of the second creation (Resurrection) with the first creation (this world).

However, this is a strange argument because, "firstly," what appears in the above verse is a rational argument and a logical deduction. Since the deniers of Resurrection said: "How does God have the power to revive decayed bones?" the Quran replies to them: "The same One who had the power to create you in the beginning also has such power later," whereas speculative analogy in Sharia rulings is never like this, because we do not have encompassment over the benefits and harms of all Sharia rulings.

"Secondly," those who forbid analogy have excepted "analogy of priority" (Qiyas al-Awlawiyyah). For example, if the Quran says: "Do not say even the slightest word of harshness to parents," we understand by way of priority that we must not cause them physical harm. The verse under discussion is of the type of "analogy of priority" and has no relation to the disputed speculative analogy. For in the beginning, when nothing existed, Allah created the earth and the dust and created man from that dust; in the Hereafter, at least the dust of the human exists. Therefore, in the Holy Quran, in verse 27 of Surah Ar-Rum, we read: وَ هُوَ الَّذِي يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ وَ هُوَ أَهْوَنُ عَلَيْهِ‌ "And it is He who begins creation; then He repeats it, and that is [even] easier for Him."

We conclude this discussion with a Hadith which stated: "How very strange is the case of the one who denies the second creation while he sees the first creation, and how very amazing is the one who believes in the second creation yet strives only for the abode of delusion (the world)." [^1]

[^1]: "Law la" is technically for "tahdid" (urging) and stimulating the performance of an action. According to some, it was originally composed of "lam" and "la," conveying the meaning of questioning and negation, and then the "mim" was changed to "waw." This term is used in a situation where an individual or individuals are negligent in performing a task, and they are told: "Why do you not do such and such?"

[^1]: "Ra'aytum" here is from "ru'yah" in the sense of "knowledge" (ilm), not observation with the eye.

[^1]: The First University, Vol. 1 (Discussion on Embryology), p. 241.

[^2]: In this regard, we have provided further explanations in Vol. 14, p. 22 onwards, under verse 5 of Surah Al-Hajj.

[^1]: According to the first interpretation, the prepositional phrase in "عَلى‌ أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ‌" relates to "qaddarna" mentioned in the previous verse, and according to the second interpretation, it relates to "masbuqin" (take note).

[^1]: Some have considered the above verse an allusion to the "metamorphosis of nations" (maskh) in this world, that Allah transformed them into forms they did not know, but this meaning is not consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse.

[^1]: This Hadith is mentioned in the commentaries of Ruh al-Bayan, Ruh al-Ma'ani, Qurtubi, and Maraghi with slight differences as a report (khabar) and without explicitly naming the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), but the appearance of their expressions is that the Hadith is from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). In the book Al-Kafi, the first part of this Hadith is narrated from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn (عليه السلام).

Ayahs

أَفَرَءَيْتُم مَّا تَحْرُثُونَ (٦٣)
Have you considered what you sow? (63)
ءَأَنتُمْ تَزْرَعُونَهُۥٓ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلزَّٰرِعُونَ (٦٤)
Is it you who make it grow, or are We the grower? (64)
لَوْ نَشَآءُ لَجَعَلْنَـٰهُ حُطَـٰمًا فَظَلْتُمْ تَفَكَّهُونَ (٦٥)
If We wish, We turn it into chaff, whereat you are left stunned [saying to yourselves,] (65)
إِنَّا لَمُغْرَمُونَ (٦٦)
‘Indeed we have suffered loss! (66)
بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ (٦٧)
No, we are deprived!’ (67)

Commentary

Is the Sower Allah or You?!

Thus far, we have studied four of the seven arguments mentioned in this Surah regarding the Resurrection.

The verses under discussion and the verses to follow point to three other arguments, each of which is an example of Allah’s infinite power in human life. One pertains to the creation of food grains, the second to "water," and the third to "fire." These three constitute the fundamental pillars of human life: plant grains are considered the most important food source for man, water is the most vital drink, and fire is the most essential means for preparing food and other life affairs.

First, He asks: "Have you ever considered what you sow?" (أَفَرَأَيْتُمْ مَا تَحْرُثُونَ).

"Is it you who cause it to grow, or are We the Grower?" (أَأَنْتُمْ تَزْرَعُونَهُ أَمْ نَحْنُ الزَّارِعُونَ). It is interesting that in the first verse, the expression "tahruthun" is used, derived from the root "harth" (on the weight of dars), which means to cultivate (scattering the seed and preparing it for growth). In the second verse, the expression "tazra'unahu" is used, derived from the root "zira'ah," which means to cause to grow.

Clearly, the work of man is only cultivation, but causing it to grow is solely the work of Allah. Therefore, it is narrated in a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said:

"None of you should say: 'I grew (zara'tu) [the crop],' but rather say: 'I cultivated (harathtu)' (for the true Grower is Allah)." [^1]

The explanation of this argument is that what man does regarding agriculture is not unlike his role in the birth of a child. He scatters a seed and steps aside. It is Allah who has created a tiny living cell within the seed which, when placed in a favorable environment, initially uses the prepared nutrients within the seed itself, sprouts, and sends out roots. Then, with amazing speed,

[^1]: The first part of the hadith is mentioned in the commentary Majma' al-Bayan under the verses discussed, and the second part is additionally narrated in Ruh al-Bayan.

it draws help from the nutrients in the soil. The vast systems and laboratories existing within the plant begin to function, creating a magnificent stir, building the stem, branches, and ears of grain. Sometimes, from a single seed, hundreds or thousands of seeds arise. [^1] Scientists say: the systems employed in the structure of a single plant are more wondrous and far more complex than the systems found in a massive industrial city with its numerous factories.

Is One who possesses such power unable to restore the dead to life?

In the next verse, to emphasize that man has no role in the growth and development of plants except for scattering the seed, He adds: "If We willed, We could surely turn it into crushed straw, so that you would be left wondering!" (لَوْ نَشَاءُ لَجَعَلْنَاهُ حُطَامًا فَظَلْتُمْ تَفَكَّهُونَ). Yes, We could send a poisonous wind that dries it up and breaks it apart before the grains are formed, or We could set a pest upon it to destroy the harvest. We could also send a swarm of locusts upon it, or strike it with a portion of a great lightning bolt, such that nothing remains of it but a handful of dried straw, leaving you in amazement and regret at the sight of it.

If you were the true growers, would these things be possible? Therefore, know that all these blessings come from elsewhere.

[^1]: Although usually in a wheat grain, multiplication up to hundreds of grains is less commonly seen, as we mentioned in the second volume of this commentary, according to the explicit testimony of the press, in one of the southern counties of Iran, very tall and heavy-eared wheat stalks were seen in some fields, where sometimes about four thousand wheat grains existed on a single stalk!

"Hutam" (حطام) from the root "hatm" (on the weight of hatm) originally means breaking something, and it is often applied to the breaking of dry objects like decayed bones or the dry stems of plants; here it refers to straw.

The possibility has also been suggested that "hutam" here refers to the rotting of seeds under the earth and their failure to sprout. [^1] "Tafakkahun" (تفكهون) is from the root "fakiha" (فاكهة) meaning fruit. Subsequently, "fakahat" was applied to joking, jesting, and telling anecdotes which are the "fruit" of social gatherings. However, this root sometimes also carries the meaning of amazement and bewilderment, and the verse under discussion is of this type.

There is also the possibility that just as a person sometimes laughs when "angry"—which is called the laughter of anger—they might also resort to jesting during severe and heavy calamities. Thus, the meaning would be jesting due to the calamity.

Yes, you would wonder and fall into bewilderment, saying: "Indeed, we are surely burdened with debt" and have lost our capital, gaining nothing (إِنَّا لَمُغْرَمُونَ). [^2] [^3]

"Nay, but we are utterly deprived and helpless" (بَلْ نَحْنُ مَحْرُومُونَ). If you were the true growers, would such a fate be possible? These

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh al-Razi under the verses discussed. [^2]: This sentence has an omitted part, and the implied meaning is: "And you say: Indeed, we are surely burdened with debt." [^3]: "Mughramun" (مغرمون) is from the root "gharamat" (غرامت) meaning to suffer loss, and to lose time and capital.

things show that all these voices are from Him. It is He who produces vibrant plants and sometimes hundreds or thousands of seeds from a single insignificant grain—plants whose seeds are food for humans, whose stalks and leaves are food for animals, and whose roots and other parts are sometimes cures for various pains.

Ayahs

أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱلْمَآءَ ٱلَّذِى تَشْرَبُونَ (٦٨)
Have you considered the water that you drink? (68)
ءَأَنتُمْ أَنزَلْتُمُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْمُزْنِ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلْمُنزِلُونَ (٦٩)
Is it you who bring it down from the rain cloud, or is it We who bring [it] down? (69)
لَوْ نَشَآءُ جَعَلْنَـٰهُ أُجَاجًا فَلَوْلَا تَشْكُرُونَ (٧٠)
If We wish We can make it bitter. Then why do you not give thanks? (70)
أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِى تُورُونَ (٧١)
Have you considered the fire that you kindle? (71)
ءَأَنتُمْ أَنشَأْتُمْ شَجَرَتَهَآ أَمْ نَحْنُ ٱلْمُنشِـُٔونَ (٧٢)
Was it you who caused its tree to grow, or were We the grower? (72)
نَحْنُ جَعَلْنَـٰهَا تَذْكِرَةً وَمَتَـٰعًا لِّلْمُقْوِينَ (٧٣)
It was We who made it a reminder and a boon for the desert-dwellers. (73)
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٧٤)
So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme. (74)

Commentary
