From Whom Are This Water and Fire?

In these verses, reference is made to the sixth and seventh proofs of the Resurrection within this section of Surah al-Waqi'ah, which demonstrate the power of Allah over all things and over the reviving of the dead.

First, He says: "Have you considered the water that you drink?" (أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ الْمَاءَ الَّذِي تَشْرَبُونَ).

"Is it you who send it down from the clouds, or is it We who send it down?" (أَأَنْتُمْ أَنْزَلْتُمُوهُ مِنَ الْمُزْنِ أَمْ نَحْنُ الْمُنْزِلُونَ). "Muzn" (on the weight of huzn), as Raghib says in al-Mufradat:

It means "bright clouds," and some have interpreted it as "rain-bearing clouds." [1] These verses place human consciences before a series of questions and extract an admission from them. In reality, He says: Have you ever thought about this water, which is the source of your life and which you drink constantly?

Who commands the sun to shine upon the surface of the oceans, and from amidst the salty and bitter waters, separates only the particles of pure, sweet water, clean from any pollution, and sends them to the sky in the form of vapor?

Who commands these vapors to join hands and become compressed, forming pieces of rain-bearing clouds?

[1]: Lisan al-Arab, root m-z-n.

Who gives the command for the winds to move, to displace the pieces of clouds, and to send them over dry and dead lands?

Who has endowed the upper layers of the atmosphere with the property that, upon cooling, they lose the ability to hold vapor, and as a result, the existing vapors descend to the earth as raindrops—soft, gentle, slow, and successive?

If for one year the sun were to go on strike, the winds were to stop moving, the upper parts of the atmosphere were to stubbornly hold onto the vapors, and the sky became stingy toward the earth such that crops and palms could not moisten their lips, all of you would perish from thirst, and your animals, gardens, and crops would wither.

Is He who has the power to provide such blessings for you through such simple means not capable of reviving the dead? This itself is a type of reviving the dead—the revival of dead lands—which is both a sign of Monotheism and the greatness of God, as well as a proof for the Resurrection and the Hereafter.

And if we see that in the above verses only drinking water is emphasized, and nothing is said about its effect on the life of animals and plants, it is because of the extraordinary importance of water for the life of man himself; furthermore, the issue of agriculture was mentioned in the preceding verses and there was no need for repetition.

Interestingly, the importance of water and its role in human life does not decrease with the passage of time and the progress of industry and human knowledge; on the contrary, industrial man has a greater need for water. Therefore, many massive industrial institutions can only operate alongside great rivers.

Finally, in the next verse, to complete this discussion, He adds: "If We willed,

We could make this palatable and sweet water bitter and salty" (لَوْ نَشَاءُ جَعَلْنَاهُ أُجَاجًا) [1] "So why do you not give thanks?" (فَلَوْلَا تَشْكُرُونَ).

Yes, if Allah willed, He would also allow the salts dissolved in the water to evaporate along with the water particles, ascending to the sky side by side with them, forming salty and bitter clouds, and falling as raindrops just as salty and bitter as seawater! But by His perfect power, He did not give this permission to the salts. Not only the salts in the water, but even harmful and bothersome microbes are not permitted to ascend to the sky with the water vapors and contaminate the raindrops. For this reason, raindrops—whenever the air is not polluted—are the purest, cleanest, and most palatable of waters.

"Ujaj" is derived from the root "ajj" (on the weight of hajj), originally taken from the "ajij" of fire, meaning its flare and burning quality. Waters that burn the mouth due to saltiness, bitterness, or heat are called ujaj.

We conclude this discussion with a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In this Hadith we read:

"When the Prophet drank water, he would say: 'Praise be to Allah who, by His mercy, gave us sweet and palatable water to drink, and did not make it salty and bitter because of our sins!'" [2]

Finally, we reach the seventh and last proof of the Resurrection in this series of verses, and that is

[1]: In this sentence, the "lam" has been omitted and is implied as "law nasha'u la-ja'alnahu."

[2]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, vol. 27, p. 148; and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, vol. 27, p. 129.

the creation of fire—the fire which is among the most important tools of human life and the most effective means in all industries. He says: "Have you considered the fire that you ignite?" (أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ النَّارَ الَّتِي تُورُونَ).

"Is it you who produced its tree, or are We the Producer?" (أَأَنْتُمْ أَنْشَأْتُمْ شَجَرَتَهَا أَمْ نَحْنُ الْمُنْشِئُونَ). "Turun" is from the root "wary" (on the weight of nafy), meaning to conceal. The fire that is hidden within fire-starting tools and is brought out through sparking is called "wary" and "iraa."

To explain: To ignite fire and create the first spark—for which today matches, lighters, and the like are used—in the past, iron and flint were sometimes utilized, striking them together to make a spark appear. However, the Arabs of Hijaz used two specific types of trees that grew in the deserts, named "Markh" and "Afar," as two fire-sticks. They would place the first underneath and strike the second upon it, and like flint and stone, a spark would be produced from it.

Most commentators have interpreted the above verses in this sense: that Allah wants to argue His ultimate power through the fire hidden in the wood of such trees used as fire-sticks—that He created fire and flame in the "green tree" (shajar akhdar), while the life of the tree is in water. Where is water and where is fire?! He who has such ability to keep this water and fire alongside each other, or rather within each other, how can He not clothe the dead in the garment of life

and revive them on the Day of Resurrection?

A similar proof for the Resurrection also appears in the final verses of Surah Ya-Sin: "He who made for you from the green tree, fire, and then from it you ignite" (Ya-Sin: 80).

However, as we explained in detail in the commentary of the aforementioned verse, this Quranic expression can refer to a more subtle proof, which is the "resurrection of energies." In other words, the discussion here is not only about the "fire-stick" but also about the "fire-catcher" itself—the wood and fuel that release all that heat and energy when burning.

To explain: It has been scientifically proven that the fire we observe today when wood burns is the same heat that the trees took from the sun over many years and stored within themselves. We think the fifty years of sunlight shining on the body of the tree has vanished, unaware that all that heat is stored in the tree, and when the spark of fire reaches the dry wood and it begins to burn, it gives back that heat, light, and energy.

That is, a resurrection and a return takes place here; dead energies are revived anew and take life, saying to us: The God who provided for our resurrection has the power to provide for the resurrection of you humans as well! (For more explanation in this regard, refer to the detailed discussion we provided in vol. 18, pp. 461 to 467).

The phrase "turun," which means igniting fire, although usually interpreted here as using a "fire-stick," there is no obstacle to it also including

the "fire-catcher" (fuel), because in any case, it is a hidden fire that becomes manifest. Of course, these two meanings are not contradictory; the first meaning is understood by the general public, and the second meaning, which is more precise, has become manifest with the passage of time and the progress of science and knowledge.

In the next verse, to emphasize the above discussions, He adds: "We have made it a reminder for everyone and a provision for the travelers" (نَحْنُ جَعَلْنَاهَا تَذْكِرَةً وَمَتَاعًا لِلْمُقْوِينَ). The return of fire from within green trees is, on one hand, a reminder of the return of the soul to lifeless bodies in the Resurrection, and on the other hand, this fire is a reminder of the fire of Hell. For according to a Hadith, the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله said:

"This fire of yours which you ignite is one part out of seventy parts of the fire of Hell!" [1] As for the expression "mata'an lil-muqwin," it is a short and meaningful reference to the worldly benefits of this fire. For there are two interpretations for the meaning of "muqwin": First, that it is from the root "qawaa" (on the weight of kitab), meaning a dry and empty desert; therefore, "muqwin" refers to those who step into deserts, and since desert-dwellers are often poor, this expression is sometimes used to mean "the poor."

The second interpretation is that it is from the root "quwwat" and means "the strong." Therefore, the said word is among the words used for two opposite meanings. [2]

[1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, vol. 9, p. 6392; and Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, vol. 27, p. 131.

[2]: Note that "mata'" is applied to any means from which a human benefits in his life.

It is true that fire and "fire-stick" trees and "fire-catching" wood are used by everyone, but because travelers needed it more than anyone else to ward off cold and cook food—especially in ancient travels by caravans—it has been emphasized.

The use of fire by the "strong" (the wealthy/powerful) is also clear due to the extensiveness of their lives, especially if we expand this discussion to today's world: how the heat resulting from various types of fires moves the industrial world and turns the massive wheels of factories. If this great flame (which all comes from trees—even the fire obtained from coal or petroleum products also returns directly or indirectly to plants) were to be extinguished one day, not only the lamp of civilization but the lamp of human life would also be extinguished.

Undoubtedly, fire is one of the most important discoveries of man, while the entire role of its creation has been undertaken by the Creator, and the role of man in it is very small and insignificant. Also, undoubtedly, from the time fire was discovered, humanity stepped into a new stage of its civilization.

Yes, the Holy Quran has alluded to all these facts in a summarized way in this one short sentence.

It is also noteworthy that in the above verse, the spiritual benefit of fire—which is the reminder of the Resurrection—is mentioned first, followed by its worldly benefit, because the former is of greater importance; rather, it constitutes the foundation and basis.

Regarding the mention of these three blessings (food grains, water, fire), an order has been observed which is a completely natural sequence: man first goes after food grains, then mixes them with water, and then cooks and prepares them for nourishment with fire!

In the last verse under discussion, as a conclusion, He says: "So since it is so, glorify the name of your Lord, the Supreme, and hold Him pure and sanctified" (فَسَبِّحْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ). [1] Yes, the God who created all these blessings, each of which is a reminder of His Monotheism, Resurrection, power, and greatness, is worthy of glorification and sanctification from any defect or deficiency.

He is both "Rabb" (Lord/Nurturer) and He is "Azim" (Supreme/Great) and Powerful. Although the addressee in this sentence is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it goes without saying that the intent is all human beings.

Key Point

Here it is necessary to mention a few meaningful Hadiths in connection with the above verses from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام:

send down the rain from the clouds, O Lord). Ayahs

۞ فَلَآ أُقْسِمُ بِمَوَٰقِعِ ٱلنُّجُومِ (٧٥)
I swear by the places where the stars set! (75)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَقَسَمٌ لَّوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عَظِيمٌ (٧٦)

إِنَّهُۥ لَقُرْءَانٌ كَرِيمٌ (٧٧)
This is indeed a noble Quran, (77)
فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مَّكْنُونٍ (٧٨)
in a guarded Book (78)
لَّا يَمَسُّهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱلْمُطَهَّرُونَ (٧٩)
—no one touches it except the pure ones— (79)
تَنزِيلٌ مِّن رَّبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٠)
sent down gradually from the Lord of all the worlds. (80)
أَفَبِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ أَنتُم مُّدْهِنُونَ (٨١)
What! Do you take lightly this discourse? (81)
وَتَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ (٨٢)
And make your denial of it your vocation? (82)

Commentary

Only the Pure May Access the Sanctuary of the Qur'an

Following the extensive discussions in the preceding verses regarding the Resurrection—supported by seven distinct arguments—these verses speak of the significance of the Holy Qur'an. This is because the issue of Prophethood and the revelation of the Qur'an, after the principles of the Origin (Monotheism) and the Resurrection, constitute the most important pillars of belief. Furthermore, the Holy Qur'an contains profound discussions regarding Monotheism and the Resurrection, and strengthening its foundations is considered a strengthening of these two principles.

First, He begins with a magnificent oath, saying: "So I swear by the positions of the stars and the places of their rising and setting!" (فَلا أُقْسِمُ بِمَواقِعِ النُّجُومِ).

Many commentators believe that "la" (لا) here is not for negation, but is "za'idah" (extra) used for emphasis, just as this expression appears in other verses of the Qur'an regarding oaths by the Day of Resurrection, the self-reproaching soul, the Lord of the easts and wests, the twilight, and the like.

Meanwhile, others consider "la" here to be a negation, indicating that the subject of the oath is too great to be sworn by, similar to everyday expressions where we might say, "I will not even swear by such-and-one subject [because its greatness is beyond an oath]."

However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate, for Allah has explicitly sworn by His own Pure Essence; are the stars greater than Him that they should be exempt from being sworn by?! Commentators have mentioned several interpretations for "Mawaqi' al-Nujum" (positions of the stars):

  1. The one mentioned above: the positions of the stars and their orbits and paths.
  2. The places of their rising and setting.
  3. The falling of the stars at the threshold of the Resurrection.
  4. Some have interpreted it solely as the setting of the stars.
  5. Some, following certain narrations, consider it a reference to the revelation of various parts of the Qur'an at different intervals of time (since "nujum," the plural of "najm," is used for things occurring gradually).

Although there is no contradiction between these meanings and all may be encompassed in the verse, the first interpretation seems the most fitting. While most people at the time of the revelation of these verses did not know the significance of this oath, it has become clear to us today that every star in the sky has a specific position, and their paths and orbits—determined by the laws of gravity and repulsion—are extremely precise and calculated.

The speed of their travel is conducted according to a specific program. While this cannot be precisely calculated for distant celestial bodies, it has been meticulously studied in the solar system, which constitutes the family of stars near us. The system of their orbits is so precise and calculated that it leaves man in awe.

When we consider that, according to the testimony of scientists, there are about one billion stars in our galaxy alone, and that there are many galaxies in the universe, each with its own specific path, we become more acquainted with the significance of this Qur'anic oath.

In the book Allah and Modern Science, we read: "Astronomers believe that these stars, which exceed billions—some of which can be seen with the naked eye, and a (much larger) portion of which cannot be seen except with telescopes—indeed, some cannot even be seen with telescopes and can only be photographed with special equipment—all of these float in their own specific orbits. There is no possibility that one of them would enter the gravitational field of another or collide with one another. In fact, such a collision would be like assuming an ocean liner in the Mediterranean Sea colliding with another ship in the Pacific Ocean while both ships are moving in the same direction at the same speed; such a possibility, if not impossible, is at least remote!" [1]

Considering these scientific discoveries regarding the state of the stars, the significance of the above oath becomes clearer.

For this reason, the next verse adds: "And indeed, it is a great oath, if you could but know" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَقَسَمٌ لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عَظِيمٌ). The expression "if you could but know" (لو تعلمون) clearly testifies that human knowledge at that time had not fully grasped this reality. This itself is considered a scientific miracle of the Qur'an. In an era when some perhaps still imagined stars to be silver nails driven into the ceiling of the sky, such a statement—especially in an environment rightly considered one of ignorance—could not possibly have originated from an ordinary human being.

Now let us see for what purpose this great oath was mentioned. The next verse pulls back the curtain, saying: "That which Muhammad (peace be upon him and his family) has brought is a Noble Qur'an" (إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ).

In this way, it responds to the stubborn polytheists who constantly insisted that these verses were a form of soothsaying, or—God forbid—delusional words, or like the poems of poets, or from the devils. It clarifies that this is a heavenly revelation and a discourse whose signs, greatness, and authenticity are manifest and apparent. Its content reflects the source of its revelation, and this matter is so evident that it requires no further explanation.

Describing the "Qur'an" as "Karim" (Noble/Generous)—considering that "Karam" regarding Allah means benevolence and bestowal, and regarding humans means possessing praiseworthy character and actions, and generally refers to great virtues [1]—also points to the outward beauties of the Qur'an in terms of the eloquence and rhetoric of its words and sentences, as well as its magnificent content. This is because it was revealed by the God who is the origin and source of every perfection, beauty, goodness, and grace.

Indeed, the Speaker of the Qur'an is Noble, the Qur'an itself is Noble, its bringer is Noble, and its objectives are Noble.

Then, moving to the second description of this Heavenly Book, it adds: "It is in a protected Book" (فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ). In that "Preserved Tablet" (Lauh al-Mahfuz), in the "Knowledge of Allah," which is protected from any error, change, or alteration. Naturally, a book that originates from such a source and whose original version is there is immune to any distortion, fault, or mistake.

[1]: Raghib in Al-Mufradat, under the root "K-R-M".

In the third description, He says: "None may touch it except the purified" (لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ). [1] Many commentators, following narrations from the Infallible Imams (peace be upon them), have interpreted this verse as the impermissibility of touching the script of the Qur'an without ritual bathing (Ghusl) or ablution (Wudu).

Meanwhile, another group considers it a reference to the purified angels who have knowledge of the Qur'an or were the intermediaries of revelation to the heart of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family)—the opposite of what the polytheists claimed: that devils had brought these words down to him!

Some also consider it a reference to the meaning that the high realities and concepts of the Qur'an are understood only by the pure. As we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 2: "This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for those conscious of Allah." In other words, at least a degree of purity in the form of a "truth-seeking" spirit is necessary to understand even its basic concepts, and the more one's purity and sanctity increase, the more one's understanding of the concepts and content of the Qur'an will grow.

However, there is no contradiction between these three interpretations, and all may be encompassed within the concept of the verse.

In the fourth and final description of the Holy Qur'an, He says: "It is a revelation from the Lord of the worlds" (تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ). [2]

[1]: "La yamassuhu" is a declarative sentence that can carry the meaning of "prohibition" or "negation." [2]: "Tanzil" here is an infinitive used as a "passive participle," meaning "that which is revealed," and serves as the predicate for an omitted subject or a subsequent predicate.

The God who is the Owner and Sustainer of all the worlds has revealed this Qur'an for the training of humans upon the pure heart of His Prophet. Just as He is the Owner and Sustainer in the world of creation (Takwin), in the world of legislation (Tashri'), everything is also from Him.

Then He adds: "Do you then treat this discourse [the Qur'an] with such descriptions as something trivial and weak?" Is it so easy for you to deny and reject it?! (أَ فَبِهذَا الْحَدِيثِ أَنْتُمْ مُدْهِنُونَ). While the signs of its truthfulness and authenticity are clearly manifest, and the Word of God must be accepted with the utmost seriousness and faced as a great reality.

"This discourse" (هذا الحديث) refers to the Qur'an, and "mudhinun" is originally from the root "duhn," meaning oil. Since oil is used to soften the skin or other objects, the word "idhan" has come to mean compromise, leniency, and sometimes weakness or a lack of serious engagement. Furthermore, since hypocrites and liars often have soft and smooth tongues, this term is occasionally used to mean denial and rejection. Both meanings are possible in the above verse. Fundamentally, a person takes seriously what they believe in; if they do not take it seriously, it is proof that they do not believe it.

In the final verse under discussion, He says: "And do you make your provision [from Allah] your denial [of Him]?" (وَ تَجْعَلُونَ رِزْقَكُمْ أَنَّكُمْ تُكَذِّبُونَ). [1]

[1]: According to this interpretation, the word "shukr" (gratitude) is omitted and implied: "And you make the [gratitude for] your provision your denial." Or "provision" is a metaphor for "gratitude for provision."

Some have said: the meaning is that your only portion from the Qur'an is denial, or that you have made denial the means of your provision and livelihood. [1] However, the first of these latter two interpretations seems more consistent with the preceding verses and is more aligned with the occasion of revelation mentioned for this verse. Many commentators have narrated from Ibn Abbas that during one of the journeys, the companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) were afflicted with severe thirst. The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) prayed, rain descended, and everyone was quenched. However, during this, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) heard a man saying: "Rain has descended by the blessing of the rising of such-and-such star!" (In the Age of Ignorance, the Arabs believed in "Anwa'," referring to stars that appeared in the sky at different intervals. The pagan Arabs believed that with the appearance of each of these stars, rain would fall, and thus they would say: "We have been given rain by the star of so-and-so." This was one of the manifestations of polytheism, idolatry, and star-worship). [2] It is noteworthy that in some narrations it is mentioned that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) rarely interpreted verses [directly], but among the instances he did interpret was this very verse, saying:

"The meaning of 'And you make your provision your denial' is that instead of giving thanks for your provisions, you deny." [3]

Key Points

1. Characteristics of the Holy Qur'an

From the four descriptions mentioned in the above verses regarding the Qur'an, it can be concluded that the greatness of the Qur'an is due, on one hand, to the greatness of its content; on the second, to the depth of its meanings; on the third, to a sanctity that only the pure and virtuous can access; and on the fourth, to its extraordinary educational and transformative aspect, as it was revealed by the Lord of the Worlds. Each of these four subjects requires detailed discussion, which we have explained under the appropriate verses.

2. The Qur'an and Purity

We read in the above verses that none touch the Qur'an except the purified. We noted that this verse has been interpreted as both physical and spiritual touching, and there is no contradiction between them; they are combined in the general concept of the verse.

Regarding the first part, it is narrated in the reports of the Ahl al-Bayt from Abu al-Hasan, Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام: "Do not touch the Mushaf (the written Qur'an) except in a state of purity, nor while in a state of ritual impurity (Janabah). Do not touch its script, nor carry it [around the neck], for Allah the Exalted says: 'None may touch it except the purified.'" [1] The same meaning is narrated in another hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام with a slight variation. [2]

[1]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 1, p. 269, Hadith 3 (According to this hadith, the negation in the verse is a metaphor for prohibition). [2]: Wasa'il al-Shi'ah, Vol. 1, p. 270, Hadith 5.

This is also found in Sunni sources; for instance, it is narrated through various paths that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "None should touch the Qur'an except one who is pure." [1] Regarding spiritual touching, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله that he said regarding: "Indeed, it is a Noble Qur'an in a protected Book," he said: "With Allah in purified pages; none may touch it except the purified," he said: "The near ones (Al-Muqarrabun)!" [2]

This matter can also be argued through reason. Although the Holy Qur'an is for the guidance of all, we know there were many who heard the Qur'an from the blessed lips of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and saw this clear water of truth at the spring of revelation, yet because they were polluted with prejudice, enmity, and stubbornness, they gained not the slightest benefit from it. However, those who purified themselves even slightly and approached it with a spirit of truth-seeking and investigation were guided. Therefore, the more a person's purity and piety increase, the deeper and more numerous the concepts of the Holy Qur'an they will attain. Thus, the verse is true in both physical and spiritual dimensions. It goes without saying that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, the Infallible Imams, and the near angels are the clearest examples of "the near ones" and understand the realities of the Qur'an better than anyone.

[1]: This hadith is narrated in Al-Durr al-Manthur from Abdullah ibn Umar, Mu'adh ibn Jabal, and Ibn Hazm al-Ansari from the Noble Prophet of Islam (ص) (Vol. 6, p. 162). [2]: Al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 162.

Ayahs

فَلَوْلَآ إِذَا بَلَغَتِ ٱلْحُلْقُومَ (٨٣)
So when it reaches the throat [of the dying person], (83)
وَأَنتُمْ حِينَئِذٍ تَنظُرُونَ (٨٤)
and at that moment you are looking on [at his bedside] (84)
وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن لَّا تُبْصِرُونَ (٨٥)
—and We are nearer to him than you are, though you do not perceive— (85)
فَلَوْلَآ إِن كُنتُمْ غَيْرَ مَدِينِينَ (٨٦)
then why do you not restore it, if you are not subject (86)
تَرْجِعُونَهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٨٧)
[to Divine dispensation], if you are truthful? (87)

Commentary

When the Soul Reaches the Throat

Among the critical moments that deeply immerse a human being in reflection is the moment of death and the end of human life. At that moment, when the matter is settled and those around the dying person look on in despair and hopelessness, they see him slowly fading away like a candle whose life has ended, bidding farewell to life, and no one can do anything about it.

Indeed, the complete weakness and helplessness of man become manifest in these critical moments. This is true not only in past times; even today, with all the technical and medical equipment and the presence of all therapeutic means, this weakness and abasement at the time of death are just as evident and clear as in the past.

In completing the discussions on the Resurrection and responding to the deniers and beliers, the Holy Quran provides a vivid depiction of this moment, saying: "Then why, when the soul reaches the throat, [do you not restore it]?" (فَلَوْ لا إِذا بَلَغَتِ الْحُلْقُومَ‌) [1]

"And you at that time are looking on [and can do nothing]" (وَ أَنْتُمْ حِينَئِذٍ تَنْظُرُونَ‌). The addressees here are those surrounding the dying person. On one hand, they observe his condition; on the other, they witness their own weakness and inability; and on a third level, they recognize the power of Allah over all things and that life and death are in His hands, and they also know that they themselves face such a destiny [2].

Then It adds: "While We are nearer to him than you, and Our angels—

[^1]: The verse has an omitted part that is understood from the subsequent verses, and the implied meaning is: "Then why, when it reaches the throat, do you not return it and you possess no power?" (The feminine form of the verb here is because it refers back to the "soul" [nafs]).

[^2]: The possibility suggested by some that the addressee here is the dying person himself seems very unlikely, as the following verse clearly clarifies that the addressees are those around him.

—who are ready to take his soul are also nearer than you, but you do not see" (وَ نَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْكُمْ وَ لكِنْ لا تُبْصِرُونَ‌).

We know well what is passing within the soul of the dying person, and what turmoil is occurring in the depths of his being. It is We who have issued the command to take his soul at a specific term, but you only see the outward signs of his condition, and you are unaware of how he is being transferred from this abode to the next, and of the severe storms raging at this moment.

Therefore, the meaning of this verse is Allah's closeness to the dying person, although some have suggested it refers to the closeness of the angels of death; however, the first interpretation is more consistent with the outward meaning of the verse.

In any case, not only at this time but at all times, Allah is closer to us than anyone else; He is even closer to us than we are to ourselves, though we are far from Him due to our ignorance. However, the manifestation and emergence of this reality are clearer at the moment of death than at any other time.

Then, for further emphasis and to clarify this same truth, It adds: "Then why, if you are not to be recompensed for your deeds..." (فَلَوْ لا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ غَيْرَ مَدِينِينَ‌).

"...do you not bring it back, if you are truthful?" (تَرْجِعُونَها إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌). This weakness and inability of yours is proof that the Master of life and death is someone else, and the reward and punishment are in His hand, and it is He who gives death and gives life.

"Madinin" (مدينين) is the plural of "Madin" (مدين) from the root "Din" (دين) meaning recompense. Some have interpreted it to mean "Marbubin" (مربوبين), meaning: if you are not under the Lordship of another and are the masters of your own affairs, then bring the soul back. This itself is proof that you are under the governance of another.

Key Points

1- The Moment of the Tyrants' Helplessness!

In reality, the purpose of these verses is to state Allah's power over the matter of life and death so that it may serve as a bridge to the issue of Resurrection. The choice of the moment of death and the deathbed here is because of the manifestation of man's complete weakness and inability at this time, despite all the power he imagines for himself.

It is not out of place here to consider the states of some tyrants when the moment of their death arrived at the height of their power, so that the depth of the meaning of these verses becomes clearer.

"Al-Mas'udi" in "Muruj al-Dhahab," regarding the states of "Al-Ma'mun" and his war with the Roman army, relates a story whose summary is as follows: When he was returning from the battlefield, he reached a spring called "Badidun" in the region known as "Qushayrah" and stopped there to rest. The purity, coldness, and brilliance of the spring water amazed him, as did the greenery, freshness, and lushness of that area. He ordered wood to be cut from the trees to build a bridge over the spring and a roof of wood and leaves above it. He rested there while the water flowed beneath his feet. The water was so clear that if a dirham was thrown into it, the inscription on it could be read from the bottom! But it was so cold that no one could dip their hand into the water.

At that moment, a relatively large fish about a cubit long appeared, looking like a piece of silver. Al-Ma'mun said, "Whoever catches it, I will give him a sword as a reward." Some servants rushed forward and caught it. When they brought it near Al-Ma'mun, the fish jerked, jumped out of the servant's hand, and fell into the water like a stone, splashing a little water on Al-Ma'mun's chest, throat, and shoulders, so that his clothes became wet. The servant went down again, caught the fish, and placed it in a handkerchief before Al-Ma'mun while it was still twitching. Al-Ma'mun said, "Fry it and prepare it now."

But suddenly a tremor fell upon his body such that he was unable to move. They covered him with multiple quilts, but he still trembled and cried out, "The cold, the cold!" They lit a fire for him, yet his cry of cold continued. They fried the fish and brought it to him, but he was not even able to taste it. When his condition worsened, he requested help from "Bakhtishu" and "Ibn Masawayh" (both court physicians of Al-Ma'mun). While he was in the throes of death, Bakhtishu took one of his hands and Ibn Masawayh took the other; they saw that his pulse was completely out of balance, signaling his demise and the disintegration of his physical system. In this state, a peculiar sweat transpired from his body that was viscous and sticky like oil! These two physicians were baffled by his condition and confessed they had never read of such a thing in medical books, but whatever it was, it was proof of his approaching death.

Al-Ma'mun's condition grew worse. He said, "Take me to a high point overlooking my army so I may observe their state." By then, night had fallen. When he looked from that point at the tents, his army, and the many fires they had lit, he said: "O He whose kingdom never vanishes, have mercy on one whose kingdom is vanishing." Then they brought him to his bed and sat someone beside him to prompt him with the Shahadatayn (the two testimonies of faith). Because his hearing had become heavy, the man raised his voice. Ibn Masawayh said, "Do not shout; by Allah, at this moment he does not distinguish between 'Allah' and 'Mani'!"

At that moment, Al-Ma'mun opened his eyes, and his pupils were so dilated and red as never before. He wanted to strike Ibn Masawayh with his hand but could not; he wanted to say something harsh but lacked the power. In that very hour, he passed away [1]. It is possible his illness had a prior history, or as some historians say, anyone who drank from that spring became ill, or the fish had some kind of toxic secretions. Whatever it was, a government and power of such magnitude collapsed in a few moments, and the hero of great battlefields knelt before death.

No one at that moment had the ability to take a step for him, or at least to bring him to his original home. History remembers many such exemplary stories.

2- Is the Departure of the Soul Gradual?

The expression of the soul reaching the throat mentioned in the above verses (فَلَوْ لا إِذا بَلَغَتِ الْحُلْقُومَ‌) is a metaphor for the final moments of life. Perhaps its origin is that most parts of the body, such as the hands and feet, cease to function before other parts at the time of death, and the throat is among the last parts to cease functioning.

In verse 26 of Surah Al-Qiyamah, we also read: كَلَّا إِذا بَلَغَتِ التَّراقِيَ‌: "No! When it [the soul] reaches the collarbones" (the "Taraqi" are the bones surrounding the throat).

[^1]: "Muruj al-Dhahab" as quoted in Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 1, p. 44.

Ayahs

فَأَمَّآ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (٨٨)
Then, if he be of those brought near, (88)
فَرَوْحٌ وَرَيْحَانٌ وَجَنَّتُ نَعِيمٍ (٨٩)
then ease, abundance, and a garden of bliss. (89)
وَأَمَّآ إِن كَانَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٩٠)
And if he be of the People of the Right Hand, (90)
فَسَلَـٰمٌ لَّكَ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٩١)
then [he will be told,] ‘Peace be on you, from the People of the Right Hand!’ (91)
وَأَمَّآ إِن كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٩٢)
But if he be of the impugners, the astray ones, (92)
فَنُزُلٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ (٩٣)
then a treat of boiling water (93)
وَتَصْلِيَةُ جَحِيمٍ (٩٤)
and entry into hell. (94)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ حَقُّ ٱلْيَقِينِ (٩٥)
Indeed this is certain truth. (95)
فَسَبِّحْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٩٦)
So celebrate the Name of your Lord, the All-supreme! (96)

Commentary

The Fate of the Righteous and the Wicked

These verses are, in fact, a summary of the opening verses of the Surah and the recent verses, illustrating the difference in the state of human beings as they stand on the threshold of death. How is it that some close their eyes to the world in ultimate peace, comfort, and joy, while another group gives up their souls with such anxiety and terror upon witnessing the prospect of the blazing fire of Hell?

First, He says: "Then if he (the dying person) is of those brought near (to Allah)..." (فَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ‌).

"...[For him is] rest and provision and a garden of pleasure" (فَرَوْحٌ وَ رَيْحانٌ وَ جَنَّةُ نَعِيمٍ‌). "Rawh" (rhyming with qawl), as linguists have stated, originally means "breathing," and "Rayhan" means a fragrant thing or plant. Subsequently, this word (rawh) was applied to anything that is a source of life and comfort, just as "Rayhan" is applied to every kind of blessing and provision that is good and joy-inducing. Therefore, divine "Rawh" and "Rayhan" encompass all means of human comfort and peace, and every kind of divine blessing and grace.

In other words, it can be said that "Rawh" refers to all matters that deliver a person from hardships so that they may draw a breath of ease, while "Rayhan" refers to the bounties and blessings that reach a person after the removal of hardships.

Islamic commentators have mentioned numerous interpretations for these two words, perhaps reaching over ten interpretations.

Sometimes it is said: "Rawh" means "mercy" and "Rayhan" includes every honor and virtue. Sometimes it is said: "Rawh" is salvation from the fire of Hell and "Rayhan" is entry into Paradise. Sometimes "Rawh" has been considered as peace in the grave and "Rayhan" as being in Paradise. Sometimes "Rawh" is interpreted as "Kashf al-Kurub" (the removal of distress) and "Rayhan" as "Ghufran al-Dhunub" (the forgiveness of sins). And sometimes "Rawh" is considered to mean "looking upon the Face of Allah" and "Rayhan" as "hearing the Speech of Allah"... and the like.

However, as we have said, these are specific instances of that general and comprehensive concept mentioned in the commentary of the verse.

It is noteworthy that after mentioning "Rawh" and "Rayhan," He speaks of "Jannat Na'im" (a Garden of Pleasure), which may be an indication that "Rawh" and "Rayhan" come to the believers at the threshold of death, and in the grave and Barzakh (the intermediate world), while Paradise is in the Hereafter. As we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of this verse:

“As for if he is of those brought near, then Rawh and Rayhan are for him,” meaning in his grave, “and a Garden of Pleasure,” meaning in the Hereafter. [^1] and [^2]

Then He adds: "And if he is of the companions of the right..." (those righteous men and women whose record of deeds is given to their right hand as a sign of victory and acceptance) (وَ أَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ‌).

"Then [the angels say], 'Peace be upon you' from your companions of the right" (فَسَلامٌ لَكَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ‌). In this way, the angels of death, at the threshold of the transition from this world, convey the greetings of his companions to him, just as we read in verse 26 of Surah al-Waqi'ah describing the people of Paradise:

إِلَّا قِيلًا سَلاماً سَلاماً.

There is another possibility in the interpretation of this verse, which is that the "Salam" (peace) is from the angels who say to him: "Peace be upon you, O you who are of the companions of the right," meaning it is enough for your honor and description that you are among them. ^3 In other verses of the Quran, the greeting of the angels to the believers at the threshold of death is also mentioned, such as verse 32 of Surah al-Nahl which says: الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ‌

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 228, Hadith 103 and 104. [^2]: "Rawh" may be the predicate (khabar) for a hidden subject (mubtada), estimated as "fajaza'uhu rawh" (his reward is rest), or it is the subject for a hidden predicate, estimated as "falahu rawh" (for him is rest). The phrase "farawhun..." is the recompense for "amma," and the conditional "in" is satisfied by this recompense (take note). ^3: Therefore, there are two estimations in the verse: "It is said to him: Peace be upon you, for you are of the companions of the right." But according to the first interpretation, there is only one estimation: "It is said to him."

“Those whom the angels take in death while they are pure, saying, 'Peace be upon you. Enter Paradise for what you used to do.'”

In any case, the expression "Salam" is a very meaningful one, whether it be from the angels or from the companions of the right; it is a peace that is a sign of "Rawh" and "Rayhan" and every kind of health, peace, and blessing. [^1] It is also necessary to remind that the expression "Companions of the Right" (those who are given their record of deeds in their right hand) is because humans usually perform important and skillful tasks with the right hand; thus, the right hand is a symbol of power, skill, ability, and victory.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding this verse, we read:

“They are our Shi’a and our lovers.” [^2]

Then He turns to the third group, who were mentioned at the beginning of the Surah as the "Companions of the Left," saying: "But if he was of the deniers [who were] astray..." (وَ أَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ الضَّالِّينَ‌).

"Then an accommodation of scalding water [is for him]"! (فَنُزُلٌ مِنْ حَمِيمٍ‌) ^3

[^1]: Regarding the greetings bestowed upon the people of Paradise, a more detailed discussion is found in Vol. 18, p. 420, under verse 58 of Surah Ya-Sin. [^2]: Tafsir al-Burhan, Vol. 4, p. 285.

"And burning in Hellfire" (وَ تَصْلِيَةُ جَحِيمٍ‌). Yes, at that very threshold of death, they taste the first of the divine punishments, and the bitter taste of the Hereafter's penalties sinks into their souls in the grave and Barzakh. Since the discourse is about the state of the dying person, it is appropriate that the phrase "accommodation of scalding water" refers to the punishment of Barzakh, and "burning in Hellfire" refers to the punishment of the Resurrection. This meaning has also been narrated in several traditions from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام. [^1] It is noteworthy that here "deniers" and "those astray" are mentioned together; the first refers to the denial of the Resurrection, the One God, and the Prophethood of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the second refers to those who have deviated from the path of truth.

This expression, besides conveying emphasis, can point to the fact that among the astray, there are those who are "Mustad'af" (oppressed/weakened) and "Jahil Qasir" (inadvertently ignorant), who hold no enmity or stubbornness against the truth; they may be subject to divine grace. However, the stubborn and antagonistic deniers will be caught in such fates as described.

"Hamim" means hot and scalding water or hot and poisonous winds, and "Tasliyah" is from the root "Sala" (rhyming with sa'y) meaning to burn and to enter the fire. However, "Tasliyah," which has multiple meanings, specifically means burning.

And at the conclusion of this discourse, He adds: "Indeed, this is the absolute truth" (إِنَّ هذا لَهُوَ حَقُّ الْيَقِينِ‌).

"So since it is so, exalt the name of your Lord, the Most Great" (فَسَبِّحِ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الْعَظِيمِ‌).

It is well-known among commentators that "Haqq al-Yaqin" is an explanatory addition (idafah bayaniyyah), meaning that what was said regarding these three groups—the Muqarrabun, the Companions of the Right, and the Deniers—is the very reality, truth, and certainty.

There is also the possibility that since certainty has levels, its highest stage is "Haqq al-Yaqin," meaning a complete, real certainty free from any doubt, ambiguity, or suspicion. [^1] Furthermore, from what we have said, it becomes clear that "this" (hadha) in this verse refers to the states of the three groups mentioned previously. Some have also suggested it refers to the entire content of Surah al-Waqi'ah or the entire Quran, but the first interpretation is more appropriate.

It is also noteworthy that the expression "fasabbih" (so glorify/exalt) with the "fa" of consequence points to the fact that what was said about these three groups is the essence of justice. Therefore, declare your Lord pure and free from any kind of oppression or injustice, or [it means] if you wish not to be caught in the fate of the third group, consider Him pure and free from any shirk (polytheism) or injustice which is the requisite of denying the Resurrection.

Many commentators have narrated under the last verse that after its revelation, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "Place it in your Ruku' (bowing)" (say: Subhana Rabbi al-Azim). And when “Exalt the name of your Lord, the Most High” (Surah al-A'la) was revealed, he said: "Place it in your Sujud (prostration)" (say: Subhana Rabbi al-A'la). [^2]

[^1]: According to this interpretation, the addition of "Haqq" to "Yaqin" is for specification and restriction. Some have also considered it as the addition of a noun to its adjective, meaning "al-Yaqin al-Haqq" (the True Certainty). [^2]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Ruh al-Ma'ani, Ruh al-Bayan, Qurtubi, Durr al-Manthur, and Tafsir al-Maraghi under the verse in question.

In the commentary of verse 74 of this same Surah, we also narrated a similar tradition from some commentators.

Key Point: The World of Barzakh

Are the above verses among those that point to the world of Barzakh? Because as we said in the commentary of these verses, at the threshold of death when a person is ready to transition to the other world, they will face one of the following states: divine blessings, bounties, rewards, and "Rawh" and "Rayhan," or painful penalties and punishments. The context within the verses shows that part of these relates to the Resurrection and another part relates to the grave and Barzakh, and this itself is considered another proof for the existence of this world.

In a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The first thing a believer is given glad tidings of at the time of death is Rawh, Rayhan, and a Garden of Pleasure. And the first thing a believer is given glad tidings of in his grave is that it is said to him: 'Glad tidings to you of the pleasure of Allah; welcome to Paradise. Allah has forgiven all those who escorted you to your grave, confirmed their testimony regarding you, and answered their prayers for your forgiveness.'" [^1] In another Hadith, the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says: "When a human is on the last day of the days of this world and the first day of the days of the Hereafter, his wealth, children, and deeds are manifested before him. He looks at his deeds and says: 'I was indifferent toward you even though you were heavy on my shoulders; what news do you have for me now?' His deed says: 'I am your companion in the grave and on the Day of Resurrection until you and I are present before your Lord.'" Then the Imam عليه السلام added: "If he is a friend of Allah, his deed appears in the form of the most fragrant human with the most beautiful face and most attractive clothing, and says: 'Glad tidings to you of peace, blessing, and a bountiful Paradise; your arrival is a blessed one.' He asks: 'Who are you?' It replies: 'I am your righteous deed that goes (with you) from the world toward Paradise.'" [^1] We had a more detailed discussion regarding the world of Barzakh under verse 100 of Surah al-Mu'minun. [^2]

O Lord! Place us in the ranks of those brought near, the companions of the right, and Your special friends and allies, and grant us "Rawh," "Rayhan," and Your "Jannat Na'im" at the threshold of death. O God! The punishment of Your Resurrection is a painful punishment that no one has the strength to endure, and Your limitless rewards are a great reward that no one deserves by their deeds alone. Our only capital on that day is Your grace and generosity, O Generous One! O Allah! Before the arrival of the Great Resurrection and the arrival of death—which is the Minor Resurrection—awaken us so that we may prepare ourselves for this great journey that lies ahead.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Waqi'ah. 1406/2/6 AH (1364/11/27 SH).
